item.title: How to Build Your Own Table When There's No Seat for You in Leadership
item.content:encoded:
What if the most powerful table you'll ever sit at is the one you build yourself?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've spent years watching brilliant women wait for permission that never comes. When I met Neetu Sadhwani, I knew immediately she was someone who refused to wait—and her story of leaving everything comfortable to build something entirely her own is exactly what we all need to hear right now.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Building your own table is more powerful than waiting for someone else to offer you a seat.
  2. Leaving comfort behind for purpose requires courage, but it's essential for authentic leadership.
  3. Burnout is often a signal that you're not aligned with your true purpose and values.
  4. Grace, grit, and vision are the three pillars needed to build something meaningful from scratch.
  5. Believing in yourself even when it's hard is a non-negotiable skill for women leaders.
  6. Real balance isn't about perfect equilibrium but about making intentional choices aligned with your priorities.
  7. Women are more capable than they think, but permission-giving starts from within.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Why waiting for a seat at the table just isn't it anymore — she built her own"

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about building your own table: it's not just about rejecting the old boys' club—it's about recognizing that the table you create will always serve you better than any seat someone else offers. In my conversation with Neetu Sadhwani, we unpacked why so many of us are still sitting in waiting rooms instead of construction zones, and honestly, it's time we changed that narrative.

Neetu walked away from comfort, and I mean real comfort—the kind that pays well and looks impressive on LinkedIn. But here's what she discovered: burnout isn't just about being tired. It's your internal GPS screaming that you're driving in the wrong direction. When you're constantly exhausted despite success, that's not a time management problem—that's a misalignment problem. Your body and soul are telling you something your logical brain doesn't want to hear yet.

We talked about the three pillars that make building from scratch not just possible, but powerful: grace, grit, and vision. Grace gives you permission to be imperfect while you figure it out. Grit keeps you moving when the blueprint gets blurry. And vision? That's what makes you magnetic to the right opportunities and people. But here's the thing—you can't outsource believing in yourself. That permission has to come from within, even when (especially when) the external validation isn't there yet.

The conversation got real about balance too, because let's be honest—the idea of perfect equilibrium is keeping us small. Real balance isn't about measuring your time with a stopwatch. It's about making intentional choices that align with what matters most to you right now, in this season. Sometimes that means your table looks different from everyone else's, and that's exactly the point.

This episode is packed with the kind of practical wisdom that shifts how you see your own capabilities, plus there's a quiz that'll help you figure out whether you're ready to start building or if you're still looking for someone else's blueprint to follow.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Neetu Sadhwani

Neetu Sadhwani is a visionary entrepreneur and leader who exemplifies building something meaningful from the ground up. Known for her courage in leaving comfort behind to chase purpose, she's a powerful voice on creating your own opportunities rather than waiting for permission.

item.title: How to Build Stronger Relationships Through Emotional Wellness and Boundaries
item.content:encoded:
What if the relationships you're struggling with aren't broken—they just need better boundaries?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about how we show up in our relationships, especially when we're juggling everything life throws at us. My conversation with licensed therapist Vanessa Morgan opened my eyes to how much our emotional wellness directly impacts every relationship we have—from our teams to our families.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Emotional wellness requires intentional management and grace toward yourself as you navigate life's complexities.
  2. Setting healthy boundaries is essential for maintaining strong relationships in both personal and professional contexts.
  3. Effective communication skills can be learned and applied to resolve conflicts more constructively.
  4. Building deeper connections requires vulnerability and the willingness to engage authentically with others.
  5. Conflict resolution is a skill that strengthens relationships when approached with the right tools and mindset.
  6. Personal growth and emotional intelligence directly impact your ability to lead and influence others.
  7. Professional therapy insights can be practically applied to everyday relationship challenges.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Managing your emotional world with grace and intention is not just possible—it's essential for thriving relationships."

The big idea

You know that feeling when a conversation at work goes sideways, or when you're having the same argument with your partner for the hundredth time? We often think the other person is the problem, but what if I told you that most relationship struggles actually stem from unclear boundaries and unmanaged emotional wellness? Vanessa Morgan, a licensed therapist who's spent years helping people navigate these exact challenges, breaks down why this happens and what you can do about it.

Here's what hit me hardest from our conversation: emotional wellness isn't about having it all figured out. It's about giving yourself grace while you intentionally manage the complexities that come with being human. Vanessa shared how this foundation directly impacts every relationship you have—whether you're leading a team meeting, negotiating with your teenager, or working through conflict with your spouse. When you understand your own emotional patterns, you show up differently in every room you enter.

The boundary piece was a game-changer for me. Vanessa explained that healthy boundaries aren't walls—they're actually what allow us to build stronger, more authentic connections. She gave us practical tools for communicating these boundaries in ways that strengthen relationships rather than damage them. This applies whether you're dealing with a colleague who constantly interrupts you or a family member who doesn't respect your time. The skills are the same, but the application changes based on the relationship.

What I love most about Vanessa's approach is how she makes conflict resolution feel doable. She shared specific communication strategies that you can use immediately—techniques that help you stay connected to the other person while still addressing the real issues. These aren't therapy-speak concepts that sound good but don't work in real life. These are practical tools that work in boardrooms and kitchens alike.

The vulnerability piece really challenged me. Vanessa talked about how building deeper connections requires us to show up authentically, which means being willing to be seen—really seen—by the people in our lives. This directly impacts your ability to lead and influence others, because people follow leaders they trust, and trust requires authenticity. If you're ready to transform how you show up in your relationships and discover your unique leadership strengths, you'll want to read the full conversation and take our leadership assessment.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Vanessa Morgan

Vanessa Morgan is a licensed marriage and family therapist with extensive professional experience in communication, conflict resolution, and relationship dynamics. She specializes in helping individuals and couples build deeper, more meaningful connections in both personal and professional settings.

item.title: How Intuitive Leadership Transforms Motherhood and Business Success
item.content:encoded:
What if the most powerful business strategy you need is already living inside you?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot lately about how we've been trained to look everywhere else for answers—experts, frameworks, best practices—when so often, our own inner wisdom knows exactly what we need. My conversation with Nikki Bostwick reminded me why trusting that voice isn't just nice-to-have, it's business-critical.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Intuitive leadership requires trusting your inner wisdom over external expectations in both business and parenting decisions.
  2. Building a wellness brand authentically means creating products and content that solve problems you personally experience.
  3. Balancing entrepreneurship and motherhood isn't about perfect harmony but about conscious choices and self-compassion.
  4. Heart-centered business approaches can lead to deeper connections with customers and more sustainable success.
  5. There's no single right way to manage multiple roles - you have permission to do it your own way.
  6. Wellness practices like mindful morning routines can anchor busy mothers and business leaders throughout chaotic days.
  7. Community and honest conversation about the messy realities of modern womanhood create powerful support systems.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The messy, beautiful truth about doing it all is that you get to do it your own way."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're standing at a crossroads—whether it's a business decision or a parenting moment—and everyone around you has an opinion about what you should do? Nikki Bostwick has built an entire wellness empire by doing the opposite: trusting her gut over the noise. As the founder of The Fullest, she's proven that intuitive leadership isn't just some woo-woo concept—it's a legitimate business strategy that can transform how you show up as both a leader and a mother.

Here's what hit me hardest in our conversation: Nikki didn't start The Fullest because she saw a market opportunity. She started it because she was personally struggling with the messy realities of modern womanhood and couldn't find products that actually addressed what she was going through. Instead of following someone else's playbook for building a wellness brand, she created solutions for problems she was living. That's intuitive leadership in action—and it's exactly why her community is so fiercely loyal.

But let's get real about what this looks like day-to-day. Nikki shared how balancing entrepreneurship and motherhood isn't about achieving some mythical perfect harmony. It's about making conscious choices moment by moment, extending yourself the same compassion you'd give your best friend, and remembering that there's no single right way to manage it all. She talks about using mindful morning routines as anchors when everything else feels chaotic—not because she read it in a book, but because her body and mind told her that's what she needed.

What I love most about Nikki's approach is how she's built genuine community around the beautiful mess of doing life your own way. She's not selling you someone else's definition of success or wellness. She's creating space for honest conversations about what actually works for real women juggling real responsibilities. Her heart-centered business philosophy has led to deeper customer connections and more sustainable growth than any traditional marketing strategy could have delivered.

This conversation will give you permission to trust your own inner compass, whether you're making a hiring decision or figuring out bedtime routines. Ready to discover what your intuition has been trying to tell you about your leadership and your life? The full episode breakdown and our exclusive quiz will help you identify your own intuitive leadership style.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Nikki Bostwick

Nikki Bostwick is the founder and editor-in-chief of The Fullest, a wellness brand known for its beloved saffron latte. She's a wellness visionary and mother who brings a heart-centered approach to both business and parenting, emphasizing intuitive leadership in all aspects of life.

item.title: How a 26-Year-Old Single Mom Is Crushing It in Tech: Hannah White's Story
item.content:encoded:
What if being a 26-year-old single mom actually makes you better at tech, not worse?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I met Hannah White and immediately knew her story needed to be shared. Here's a woman who's rewriting the rules about what success looks like in your twenties, proving that life's curveballs don't derail ambition—they sharpen it. Her perspective on turning challenges into competitive advantages will shift how you see your own path.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Success in your twenties doesn't have to look like traditional career paths - you can build something meaningful while navigating life's unexpected turns.
  2. Single motherhood can actually strengthen your professional skills by forcing you to become more efficient, organized, and decisive.
  3. Technology and the right systems can be game-changers for managing both work responsibilities and family life effectively.
  4. Owning your power starts with accepting your current circumstances rather than waiting for perfect conditions to pursue your goals.
  5. Building a career in tech is possible at any life stage, even when balancing significant personal responsibilities.
  6. Setting boundaries between work and family time is crucial for maintaining both professional growth and quality parenting.
  7. Finding your identity as both a professional and a mother requires intentional choices about how you spend your time and energy.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You don't have to wait for the perfect moment to own your power - you can start exactly where you are right now."

The big idea

Hannah White is living proof that the narrative about needing perfect conditions to build a career is complete nonsense. At 26, she's raising two boys as a single mom while crushing it in the tech world, and honestly? She's doing it better than most people with half her responsibilities. This conversation will make you rethink everything you thought you knew about timing, readiness, and what it actually takes to succeed.

What struck me most about Hannah is how she's flipped the script on single motherhood. Instead of seeing it as a limitation, she's recognized it as her secret weapon. Think about it—when you're managing the chaos of raising kids solo, you become a master of efficiency, organization, and quick decision-making. Those aren't just parenting skills; they're executive skills that make you invaluable in the workplace. Hannah breaks down exactly how she's leveraged technology and systems to manage both her professional responsibilities and family life without losing her mind.

We dive deep into the messy reality of building your identity when you're wearing multiple hats. Hannah gets real about the intentional choices she makes with her time and energy, and why setting non-negotiable boundaries between work and family isn't just nice-to-have—it's survival. She's learned that you can't pour from an empty cup, but more importantly, she's figured out how to keep filling that cup even when life feels overwhelming.

The biggest takeaway? Stop waiting for permission or perfect circumstances to go after what you want. Hannah's journey shows that accepting where you are right now—not where you think you should be—is the first step to owning your power. Whether you're navigating unexpected life changes, considering a career pivot, or feeling behind compared to your peers, this episode will remind you that your timeline is your own. Ready to discover which leadership style matches your current season of life and how to leverage it for maximum impact?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Hannah White

Hannah White is a 26-year-old IT professional at Tomahawk and single mother of two boys. She's navigating the tech industry while balancing the demands of motherhood, demonstrating resilience and determination in building her career. Hannah is also Aimee Rickabus's stepdaughter, bringing a personal perspective to this candid conversation about modern motherhood and professional ambition.

item.title: How CrossFit Champion Kelly Abney Built Her Athletic Empire While Breaking Barriers
item.content:encoded:
What if the biggest barrier to your success isn't your competition—it's you minimizing your own strength?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about what it means to truly own your power, especially after my conversation with CrossFit Games champion Kelly Abney. She's built an incredible athletic empire, and the way she talks about strength—both physical and mental—completely shifted how I think about leadership.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Success in athletics and business requires embracing both your strength and vulnerability as a woman.
  2. The unseen hustle behind women's achievements often involves navigating unique challenges that men don't face.
  3. Building an empire means creating systems that support your values while scaling your impact.
  4. Grit isn't just about pushing through—it's about strategic persistence aligned with your goals.
  5. Breaking barriers requires you to own your space unapologetically while lifting others up.
  6. Balance isn't about perfection—it's about intentional choices that align with your priorities.
  7. Your strength as a woman isn't something to minimize—it's your competitive advantage.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Your strength as a woman isn't something to minimize—it's your competitive advantage."

The big idea

When Kelly Abney steps into a room, you feel her presence. But what struck me most about our conversation wasn't just her impressive athletic achievements—it was how she's learned to embrace every aspect of her strength without apology. As women, we're conditioned to make ourselves smaller, to downplay our capabilities, to soften our edges. Kelly's journey shows us what happens when you refuse to do that.

Here's what most people don't see about building an empire, whether it's athletic or business: the unsexy, unglamorous work that happens behind the scenes. Kelly shared how she's had to navigate challenges that her male counterparts simply don't face—from proving she belongs in spaces where women weren't traditionally welcome, to building systems that honor her values while scaling her impact. The grit required isn't just about pushing through pain or obstacles; it's about strategic persistence that aligns with your bigger vision.

One thing Kelly said that stopped me in my tracks: 'Balance isn't about doing everything perfectly—it's about making intentional choices that reflect your priorities.' This isn't about having it all figured out. It's about owning your decisions and creating a life that works for you, not against you. She's mastered the art of breaking barriers while lifting others up, proving that success doesn't have to be a zero-sum game.

The most powerful moment in our conversation came when Kelly talked about vulnerability as strength. In athletics and business, we often think we need to be invincible. But Kelly's learned that embracing both your power and your humanity isn't contradictory—it's revolutionary. Your strength as a woman isn't something to minimize or explain away; it's your competitive advantage. Ready to discover how Kelly's approach to owning her power can transform your leadership style and help you build your own empire?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kelly Abney

Kelly Abney is a CrossFit Games champion and founder of Abney Athletics. She's built a powerful presence in both competitive fitness and business, breaking barriers for women in athletics while creating her own empire.

item.title: From Grief to Grit: How This Widow Turned Loss Into Leadership Success
item.content:encoded:
What if being underestimated as a grieving widow actually became your greatest business advantage?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've had countless conversations about resilience, but Leslie Christen's story stopped me in my tracks. Her journey from devastating loss to building a thriving business while raising kids solo isn't just inspiring—it's a masterclass in turning your supposed 'weaknesses' into unshakeable strengths.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. The invisible labor of motherhood becomes even more complex when you're carrying it all alone after loss.
  2. Being underestimated in business can actually become your secret weapon for success.
  3. Technology and AI tools can be life-saving resources for overwhelmed mothers and entrepreneurs.
  4. Sisterhood and community support are essential for women rebuilding their lives after tragedy.
  5. Leading with soul and authenticity creates deeper connections than traditional business approaches.
  6. Resilience isn't about being strong all the time—it's about finding ways to keep moving forward.
  7. The patriarchal business world often undervalues women's emotional intelligence and heart-centered leadership.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Resilience isn't about being strong all the time—it's about finding ways to keep moving forward."

The big idea

When Leslie Christen lost her husband, she didn't just lose her partner—she lost the invisible support system that made everything else possible. Suddenly, she was carrying the full weight of motherhood, business ownership, and rebuilding her entire life while the world watched to see if she'd crumble. Instead of breaking, she discovered something powerful: being underestimated was about to become her secret weapon.

Leslie's story cuts straight to the heart of what so many of us face—that crushing invisible labor that keeps families and businesses running, but gets dismissed as 'just what moms do.' When you're carrying it all alone after loss, every decision becomes magnified. But here's what Leslie figured out: the emotional intelligence and systems thinking that motherhood demands? Those aren't soft skills—they're business superpowers that the traditional corporate world completely undervalues.

What I love about Leslie's approach is how she's leveraged technology and AI tools not as replacements for human connection, but as lifelines for overwhelmed mothers and entrepreneurs. She's proof that you don't have to choose between leading with your heart and building something profitable. Her soul-centered leadership style creates the kind of deep connections that traditional business approaches can't touch, and her clients feel it immediately.

The conversation gets real about how sisterhood and community support aren't just nice-to-haves when you're rebuilding after tragedy—they're survival tools. Leslie's journey shows us that resilience isn't about being strong all the time or pretending you've got it all figured out. It's about finding ways to keep moving forward even when you're scared, exhausted, and carrying more than anyone should have to carry alone. Her story will challenge everything you think you know about strength, success, and what it really takes to lead through the hardest seasons of life.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Leslie Christen

Leslie Christen is a powerhouse business owner, mother of two boys, and widow who has transformed personal loss into entrepreneurial strength. As one of the original founding members of the Office Hours Girls Club, she exemplifies how women can rise above grief and doubt to lead with both soul and strength.

item.title: How to Turn Dreams into Bold Goals and Live with Purpose as a Woman Leader
item.content:encoded:
What if your biggest dreams aren't too ambitious—they're just waiting for you to get specific?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, Hey friend, I had such an incredible conversation with entrepreneur and mom Wendee Close about turning those beautiful, swirling dreams in your head into concrete goals that actually move the needle. If you've ever felt torn between dreaming big and making it happen, this episode is exactly what you need.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Bold goal-setting requires transforming dreams into specific, actionable steps that align with your values and purpose.
  2. Successful women leaders balance family and business by creating systems and being intentional about their time and energy.
  3. Supporting other women-owned businesses creates a powerful network that benefits everyone in the community.
  4. Living life on purpose means making decisions that align with your core values, even when it's challenging.
  5. WBENC certification opens doors for women entrepreneurs and creates opportunities for meaningful business connections.
  6. Motherhood and entrepreneurship can complement each other when you embrace both roles authentically.
  7. Setting boundaries around your goals helps you stay focused on what truly matters most.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"When you transform your dreams into actionable steps, you're not just setting goals—you're designing the life you want to live."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you have this amazing vision for your life and business, but it feels so big and overwhelming that you don't even know where to start? That's exactly what we dig into in this conversation with Wendee Close, and honestly, her approach to transforming dreams into bold, actionable goals is going to shift how you think about planning your future.

Wendee doesn't just talk about goal-setting—she lives it. As both a successful entrepreneur and intentional mother, she's figured out how to balance the beautiful chaos of family life with building something meaningful in business. What I love most about her approach is how she makes decisions through the lens of her core values, even when it's the harder path. She talks about creating systems that actually work for real life, not just the highlight reel version we see on social media.

We also explore something that's close to my heart: how supporting other women-owned businesses creates this incredible ripple effect in our communities. Wendee shares her experience with WBENC certification and how it opened doors she didn't even know existed. But here's what really struck me—she talks about embracing both motherhood and entrepreneurship authentically, without trying to be perfect at either one. It's about being intentional with your time and energy, and honestly, that's permission we all need to hear.

The conversation takes a beautiful turn when we discuss living life on purpose versus just going through the motions. Wendee's insights about setting boundaries around your goals—not just your time, but your actual goals—is something I'm still thinking about days later. She helps you understand that when you get specific about what you want and why it matters, those dreams stop being wishes and start becoming your reality. Trust me, you're going to want to hear exactly how she breaks this down.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Wendee Close

Wendee Close is an entrepreneur, mother of two, and founder of Goals2Life.com. As a WBENC-certified business owner, she is passionate about empowering others to set bold goals and transform their dreams into actionable steps while supporting women-owned businesses across the country.

item.title: The Hidden Leadership Skills Every Woman Already Possesses
item.content:encoded:
What if everything you think isn't leadership... actually is?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been wanting to do this solo episode for months because I keep hearing the same thing from incredible women: 'I'm not really a leader.' Friend, I'm calling BS on that story. It's time we had an honest conversation about the leadership you're already demonstrating every single day.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. The invisible work women perform daily—organizing, coordinating, emotional labor—is actually sophisticated leadership in action.
  2. Leadership skills don't only develop in corporate boardrooms; they're honed through the complex demands of managing family life.
  3. Recognizing your existing leadership abilities is the first step to claiming your power in all areas of life.
  4. The juggling act of motherhood, marriage, and career teaches invaluable lessons about prioritization and decision-making.
  5. Women need to start giving themselves credit for the incredible management skills they demonstrate every single day.
  6. The journey from breakdown to breakthrough often begins with acknowledging the leadership you're already demonstrating.
  7. Self-growth and family life aren't competing priorities—they can fuel and strengthen each other when properly balanced.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The invisible work we do is leadership, and it's time we start treating it like it is."

The big idea

You know that moment when you're coordinating carpool schedules while mentally planning dinner, responding to work emails, and remembering that your daughter needs her permission slip signed by tomorrow? Most women call that Tuesday. I call it sophisticated project management that would make any Fortune 500 executive jealous.

Here's what I've learned after building a nine-figure company while raising six kids: the invisible work you do daily isn't just busy work—it's leadership in its purest form. When you're managing competing priorities, mediating sibling conflicts, anticipating needs before they become crises, and keeping multiple people happy and on track, you're demonstrating skills that business schools spend years trying to teach. The difference? You learned it in the trenches of real life, where the stakes are people you love, not just profit margins.

I shared some raw stories in this episode about my own journey from feeling like I was barely keeping my head above water to recognizing that I was actually orchestrating something incredible. The breakdown moments, the breakthrough realizations, and everything in between. Because here's the truth: the juggling act of motherhood, marriage, and career isn't something that holds you back from leadership—it's what makes you a better leader than someone who's only operated in one dimension.

The emotional labor you carry, the way you read a room and adjust your approach, how you prioritize ruthlessly when everything feels urgent—these aren't soft skills, they're power skills. They're the exact capabilities that drive results in any context, whether you're running a household or running a company. The problem isn't that you lack leadership abilities; it's that you've been conditioned not to see them, name them, or claim credit for them.

This episode is about changing that narrative once and for all. It's about recognizing that self-growth and family life aren't competing forces, but complementary strengths that can fuel each other when you stop apologizing for who you are and start leveraging what you've already mastered. Ready to see your daily reality through a completely different lens and discover exactly what type of leader you already are?

item.title: How a Leading Functional Medicine Doctor Balances Healing Others and Motherhood
item.content:encoded:
What if your gut health is the missing piece in your ability to show up powerfully as both leader and mother?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've always been fascinated by women who manage to excel in healing professions while juggling the chaos of motherhood. When I met Dr. Sunny Raleigh, a functional medicine doctor raising three boys across multiple states while running a thriving practice, I knew I had to dig deeper into how she makes it all work.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. The gut-brain connection plays a crucial role in healing chronic conditions and overall wellness.
  2. Distance parenting and co-parenting can work when you're intentional about showing up fully for your children.
  3. Small, consistent health changes create more impact than dramatic overhauls.
  4. Supporting concussion recovery requires a holistic approach that addresses both physical and neurological healing.
  5. You can run a successful medical practice while managing family responsibilities across multiple states.
  6. Treating patients that traditional medicine overlooks requires both medical expertise and deep compassion.
  7. Being a working mother means making tough choices but staying committed to both your mission and your family.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"I treat the patients no one else can—those suffering from conditions that traditional medicine often overlooks."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're running on empty but still trying to pour into everyone else? Dr. Sunny Raleigh gets it. As a functional medicine doctor and mother of three boys, she's discovered something revolutionary: the connection between your gut health and your ability to manage stress, think clearly, and show up powerfully in every area of your life isn't just medical theory—it's the foundation of sustainable success.

What struck me most about Sunny's approach is how she's redefined what it means to be present as a mother while building something meaningful. She's mastered the art of distance parenting and co-parenting by being completely intentional about the time she does have with her boys. Instead of guilt-tripping herself about not being the traditional always-available mom, she's created a model where quality trumps quantity every single time. Her kids are thriving, and so is her practice.

Here's where it gets really interesting: Sunny specializes in treating patients that traditional medicine has given up on—people with chronic conditions, concussion recovery, and complex health puzzles that require looking at the whole person, not just symptoms. She's built her entire practice around the gut-brain connection, and the results she's seeing are nothing short of transformative. But here's what she's learned that applies to all of us: small, consistent changes in how you fuel your body create exponentially more impact than dramatic overhauls that you can't sustain.

The parallel between healing others and managing your own life as a working mother is striking. Sunny approaches both with the same holistic mindset—addressing root causes instead of just managing symptoms, whether that's in her medical practice or in how she structures her family life across multiple states. She's proof that you don't have to choose between your mission and your family; you just have to get intentional about how you show up for both.

This conversation will shift how you think about the mind-body connection and give you a completely new framework for sustainable success that doesn't require you to sacrifice your health or your family. Ready to discover which of these insights could transform how you manage your own energy and impact?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Mary "Sunny" Raleigh

Dr. Mary "Sunny" Raleigh is a renowned functional medicine doctor and founder of Restoration Healthcare in Irvine, California. She specializes in treating complex chronic conditions like Lyme disease and autoimmune disorders that traditional medicine often overlooks, and is a mother of three teenage boys.

item.title: How to Balance Creative Passion with Parenting: Lessons from Musician Stacy Clark
item.content:encoded:
What if choosing between your creative dreams and motherhood is actually a false choice that's keeping you small?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking about this conversation with Stacy Clark for weeks now. As someone who's built a company while raising six kids, I thought I had the work-life balance thing figured out — but Stacy's perspective on maintaining creative identity while champering her children's dreams in Hollywood? That hit different.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. You can maintain your creative identity while fully embracing motherhood and supporting your children's dreams.
  2. The entertainment industry requires strong advocacy skills when managing young actors and musicians.
  3. Music education is crucial for child development and deserves more support in our school systems.
  4. Purpose-driven work often emerges from personal experiences and challenges you've overcome.
  5. Balancing multiple roles requires clear boundaries and intentional time management.
  6. Supporting your children's careers doesn't mean sacrificing your own professional identity.
  7. Creative work can be a powerful tool for social change and community impact.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You don't have to choose between being a creative and being a mom—you can be both beautifully."

The big idea

You know that voice in your head that whispers you have to choose? The one that says you can't be a devoted mother AND pursue your creative passions with full intensity? Stacy Clark's story is about to shut that voice down for good. This indie musician turned Hollywood advocate has cracked the code on something most of us struggle with daily — how to honor both your creative identity and your role as a mother without compromising either.

What struck me most about Stacy's journey isn't just that she's managed to keep her music career alive while navigating the intense world of managing young actors and musicians in Hollywood. It's how she's reframed the entire conversation. Instead of seeing motherhood as something that limits her creativity, she's discovered how supporting her children's dreams has actually expanded her own purpose and professional impact. The advocacy skills she's developed, the industry knowledge she's gained, the community she's built around music education — none of this happened despite being a mother. It happened because of it.

But here's where Stacy gets really practical, and why you need to hear this conversation. She's not just philosophizing about balance — she's living it with clear boundaries and intentional time management strategies that actually work. She talks openly about how she maintains her professional identity while championing her children's careers, and how that balance has led to purpose-driven work that's creating real change in music education. This isn't about having it all — it's about redefining what 'all' actually means for you.

The most powerful moment in our conversation came when Stacy shared how her personal challenges became the foundation for her community impact work. It reminded me that our struggles aren't just obstacles to overcome — they're often the very experiences that prepare us for our greatest contributions. If you've ever felt torn between your creative dreams and your responsibilities as a mother, this episode will give you a completely new framework for thinking about both roles. Head over to read the full breakdown and take our quiz to discover your own creative-parenting integration style.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Stacy Clark

Stacy Clark is an acclaimed indie musician who has successfully transitioned into managing her children's film and television careers in Hollywood. She's also a passionate advocate for music education, founding a program dedicated to bringing music back into schools across the country.

item.title: From Hollywood Makeup Artist to Clean Beauty Pioneer: Laura Linsenmeyer's Story
item.content:encoded:
What if leaving your dream job in Hollywood was actually the first step toward building your real legacy?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about the courage it takes to walk away from what looks like success to everyone else. When I sat down with Laura Linsenmeyer, I was struck by how she traded the glamour of Hollywood makeup artistry for something that mattered more to her soul—and built a movement in the process.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Transitioning from a creative career to entrepreneurship requires courage to follow your values over security.
  2. Clean beauty isn't just about products—it's about creating a movement toward conscious living.
  3. Building a purpose-driven business means prioritizing community and values alongside profit.
  4. Motherhood can amplify your mission and drive you to create positive change in the world.
  5. Leading a woman-powered brand requires intentional cultivation of supportive relationships.
  6. Authenticity in business comes from aligning your personal values with your professional mission.
  7. Success in conscious entrepreneurship is measured by impact, not just revenue.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Clean beauty isn't just about what we put on our skin—it's about the values we choose to live by"

The big idea

You know that moment when you realize the career you thought you wanted is actually keeping you from who you're meant to become? Laura Linsenmeyer lived that moment when she walked away from her thriving Hollywood makeup career to launch ROOTS the Beauty Underground. What she discovered wasn't just a business opportunity—it was a calling to create the kind of clean beauty brand that puts values before vanity and community before profit margins.

Here's what I love about Laura's story: she didn't just switch industries, she completely redefined what success looks like. Instead of chasing the next celebrity client or blockbuster film, she chose to build something that aligned with her deepest values around health, authenticity, and conscious living. This isn't about trading one hustle for another—it's about having the courage to build a business that reflects who you actually are, not who you think you should be.

The conversation gets really interesting when Laura talks about how motherhood amplified her mission. Suddenly, creating clean, safe products wasn't just good business—it became personal. She's proof that you can build a woman-powered brand that prioritizes relationships and community without sacrificing profitability. Her approach to leadership is intentional, authentic, and refreshingly honest about what it actually takes to create positive change in an industry that's not always known for putting people first.

What struck me most was Laura's perspective on measuring success. Revenue matters, but impact matters more. She's building something that will outlast her, something that changes how women think about beauty, wellness, and what they're willing to accept in the products they put on their bodies. If you've ever wondered what it looks like to lead with purpose while still building a profitable business, this episode will give you the roadmap—and probably the permission you've been waiting for to make that bold move you've been considering.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Laura Linsenmeyer

Laura Linsenmeyer is a clean beauty pioneer and founder of ROOTS the Beauty Underground, based in Laguna Beach. A former film and TV makeup artist, she transitioned from Hollywood to entrepreneurship to create a purpose-driven, non-toxic beauty brand that champions community and conscious living.

item.title: From Creative Risks to Purpose-Driven Business: Sybil Steele's Journey
item.content:encoded:
What if everything you thought you knew about your career path was actually holding you back from your real purpose?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot lately about how we define ourselves by our work, and what happens when that definition no longer fits. When Sybil Steele shared her journey from filmmaker to wellness entrepreneur, I recognized something so many of us face—the courage it takes to completely reimagine who we are professionally.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Personal transformation and motherhood can be powerful catalysts for entrepreneurial pivots and finding your true purpose.
  2. Showing up as your whole authentic self in business creates deeper connections and more meaningful work.
  3. Building businesses rooted in wellness and self-care rituals can transform both your life and your customers' experiences.
  4. Community-inspired solutions like local payment apps can address real-world problems while fostering connection.
  5. Balancing multiple ventures requires intentional focus on what truly matters and aligns with your values.
  6. Creative risks become less scary when they're aligned with your deeper sense of purpose and intuition.
  7. The journey of reinvention is ongoing and requires courage to let go of previous identities that no longer serve you.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The creative process isn't just about making something beautiful—it's about showing up as your whole self and doing work that matters."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're successful at something, but deep down you know it's not quite right anymore? That's exactly where Sybil Steele found herself as a creative entrepreneur in film. What she did next—and how she navigated multiple complete career pivots—offers a masterclass in trusting your intuition even when everyone else thinks you've lost your mind. Her story isn't just about changing careers; it's about permission to evolve as a whole person, especially when motherhood shifts everything you thought you knew about yourself.

Here's what struck me most about Sybil's approach: she didn't just switch industries, she completely redefined what work could mean in her life. When she moved into wellness and purpose-driven business, she brought her whole self—including the messy, uncertain, still-figuring-it-out parts. This authenticity became her secret weapon, creating deeper connections with her community and customers than any polished persona ever could. She's proof that showing up as yourself, even when you're mid-transformation, can be your greatest business asset.

The practical side of her journey is equally fascinating. Sybil built multiple ventures rooted in wellness and self-care, but she also tackled real-world problems like creating local payment apps that foster community connection. What I love about her approach is how she balances ambition with intentionality—she's not building businesses just to build them, but because they align with her deeper sense of purpose and values. This isn't about having it all figured out; it's about making decisions from a place of inner knowing rather than external pressure.

Perhaps most importantly, Sybil's story reminds us that reinvention isn't a one-time event—it's an ongoing practice of courage. The woman who started in film isn't better or worse than the wellness entrepreneur she became; they're both part of a continuous evolution. If you've ever felt stuck between who you were and who you're becoming, or if you're wondering whether it's too late to make a major change in your career or business, this conversation will give you both the permission and practical insights you need to take that first brave step forward.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Sybil Steele

Sybil Steele is a powerhouse creative entrepreneur who has evolved from filmmaker and global brand consultant to wellness pioneer. She's the Founder of S'eau Prima skincare, Co-Founder of Repegs payment app and Solento Tequila, and a mother raising two young adults.

item.title: How Brooke Briggs Built Two Heart-Centered Businesses While Raising Kids
item.content:encoded:
What if building a business around your kids' needs wasn't a limitation—but your greatest competitive advantage?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've always been fascinated by women who find ways to weave their business dreams into the fabric of motherhood rather than treating them as competing forces. When I sat down with Brooke Briggs, I discovered someone who didn't just balance these worlds—she made them fuel each other in the most beautiful way.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Passion and purpose can grow side by side when you align your business with your values.
  2. Building community requires invisible labor that often goes unrecognized but creates lasting impact.
  3. Sensory-rich learning and outdoor play are essential for children's development in today's digital world.
  4. You can successfully balance motherhood and business ownership when your work feels meaningful.
  5. Creating connections to nature and rhythm helps children develop authentic relationships with themselves and others.
  6. Family bonding happens naturally when parents and children engage in shared learning experiences.
  7. Purpose-driven businesses that serve community needs can thrive while making a difference.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Brooke's work is rooted in connection — to nature, to rhythm, and to the soul of childhood."

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about building a purpose-driven business: the invisible work of creating community often matters more than your marketing strategy. Brooke Briggs learned this firsthand when she transtransitioned from traditional music classes to creating something entirely her own—businesses that put children's development at the center while honoring what she knew mothers really needed.

What struck me most about Brooke's journey wasn't just her success, but how she refused to accept the false choice between meaningful work and present motherhood. Instead of fighting against her season of life with young children, she built around it. Her music classes evolved into farm-based learning experiences because she recognized something profound: children today are hungry for sensory-rich experiences and authentic connections to nature that our digital world simply cannot provide.

The magic happens when you stop trying to separate your values from your business model. Brooke discovered that rhythm and outdoor play aren't just nice additions to childhood—they're essential elements that help children develop genuine relationships with themselves and others. When parents engage in these shared learning experiences alongside their children, family bonding becomes natural rather than forced. This insight didn't just shape her programming; it became the foundation for a thriving community of families who found exactly what they didn't know they were looking for.

What I love about Brooke's approach is how she demonstrates that purpose-driven businesses can absolutely thrive financially while making a real difference. She proves that when you align your work with your deepest values, passion and profit don't compete—they amplify each other. Her story offers a roadmap for any woman who's ever wondered if she has to choose between building something meaningful and building something sustainable.

This conversation will challenge how you think about the intersection of motherhood and entrepreneurship, and it might just give you permission to build the business that's been quietly calling your name. Ready to discover which type of purpose-driven leader you are and how to leverage that in your own journey?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Brooke Briggs

Brooke Briggs is an educator and entrepreneur who founded both OC Farm School and Magic Steps Music. She specializes in creating meaningful, nature-based learning experiences that foster child development and strengthen family connections through sensory-rich education and outdoor play.

item.title: How to Build Unshakable Confidence and Rise Up Without Permission
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that confidence isn't something you're born with—it's actually a skill you can master?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to unlearn everything I thought I knew about confidence when I started scaling my company. For years, I waited for someone to give me permission to lead boldly, until I realized that permission was never coming. That's exactly what Nada Lena Nasserdeen and I dive into in this conversation.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Confidence is a learnable skill, not an innate personality trait that you either have or don't have.
  2. Modern leadership requires authenticity and the courage to bridge the gap between who you are and who you're meant to be.
  3. Overcoming imposter syndrome starts with shifting your mindset and recognizing your inherent worth and capabilities.
  4. Rising up in leadership means stopping playing small and stepping into your purpose without waiting for permission.
  5. Building unshakable confidence requires consistent practice and the willingness to embrace discomfort as part of growth.
  6. Visibility and leadership challenges are shared experiences among women that can be overcome with the right strategies.
  7. Your diverse background and experiences are assets that shape your unique vision and leadership approach.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Confidence is a skill, not a personality trait — and leadership begins when we stop playing small and start rising into our purpose."

The big idea

Here's the truth nobody talks about: that voice in your head whispering 'who do you think you are?' isn't protecting you—it's keeping you small. When I sat down with executive coach Nada Lena Nasserdeen, we peeled back the layers on why so many of us wait for permission to step into our power, and more importantly, how to stop doing it.

The biggest breakthrough in our conversation was realizing that confidence isn't this magical personality trait some people are born with while others aren't. It's a skill, just like learning to drive or mastering Excel. You can develop it through practice, even when—especially when—it feels uncomfortable. Nada shared how she helps her clients shift from 'I don't belong here' to 'I'm exactly where I need to be,' and the strategies she uses are game-changers.

We talked about imposter syndrome, but not in the usual 'fake it till you make it' way. Instead, we explored how your diverse experiences and background aren't things to overcome—they're your competitive advantage. Every challenge you've navigated, every time you've had to figure it out as you went, that's all data proving you belong in leadership. The key is recognizing that the gap between who you are today and who you're meant to become isn't evidence of inadequacy—it's your growth edge.

What really hit home for me was Nada's perspective on visibility. So many of us struggle with putting ourselves out there, whether it's speaking up in meetings, pitching ideas, or taking on stretch assignments. But leadership requires visibility, and visibility requires courage. The women who rise aren't the ones without fear—they're the ones who feel the fear and show up anyway.

This conversation will shift how you think about your own capabilities and give you practical tools to build the kind of confidence that doesn't waver when things get tough. Ready to discover your confidence-building style and get personalized strategies for stepping into your leadership without waiting for anyone's permission?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Nada Lena Nasserdeen

Nada Lena Nasserdeen is the Founder & CEO of Rise Up For You, author, executive coach, and former college professor. She is a global advocate for leadership, confidence, and self-worth who has helped thousands of professionals rise into their full potential from the classroom to the boardroom.

item.title: From Creative Chaos to Intentional Leadership: Lindsay Buwalda's Journey
item.content:encoded:
What if being underestimated as a woman in business is actually your secret weapon?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to share Lindsay Buwalda's story with you because she's living proof that creative chaos can become your greatest strength. Her journey from feeling overlooked to building an empire while raising kids hit me right in the heart—and I know it will resonate with you too.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. The invisible labor women carry extends far beyond household tasks into emotional and logistical work that often goes unrecognized.
  2. Being underestimated as a woman in business can actually become a strategic advantage when you learn to leverage it.
  3. Technology can be a powerful equalizer for creative entrepreneurs juggling multiple roles and responsibilities.
  4. Personal influence grows when you embrace intentional design in both business and life decisions.
  5. Creative leadership requires courage to rise above traditional expectations and forge your own path.
  6. Balancing motherhood and entrepreneurship means accepting that perfection isn't the goal—progress is.
  7. Building a sustainable creative business requires setting clear boundaries and protecting your energy for what matters most.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Your work matters, your leadership counts, and your creativity can change lives."

The big idea

You know that feeling when everyone assumes you're 'just the creative one' and not the business strategist? Lindsay Buwalda knows it intimately. In our conversation, she shared how she transformed from being the underestimated woman in the room to leveraging that perception as her competitive advantage. Here's what she discovered: when people don't see you coming, you have the freedom to innovate without their limiting expectations weighing you down.

We dove deep into the invisible labor that's been silently draining your energy—and it's not just about who's doing the dishes. Lindsay opened my eyes to how we're carrying the emotional and logistical weight of entire households and businesses, often without even realizing it. She's learned to name this work, value it, and most importantly, systematize it using technology as her secret weapon. The tools and strategies she shared aren't just time-savers; they're life-changers for creative entrepreneurs juggling multiple roles.

What struck me most about Lindsay's approach is her intentional design philosophy. She's not just running a business—she's architecting a life that works for her family while building something meaningful. We talked about the courage it takes to rise above traditional expectations and forge your own path as a creative leader. Lindsay shared specific examples of how she's set boundaries to protect her energy and focus on what truly moves the needle, both in her business and her relationships.

The motherhood piece of this conversation will hit you right where you live. Lindsay's honest about the reality that perfection isn't the goal—progress is. She's building a sustainable creative business while raising kids, and her strategies for balancing it all are both practical and inspiring. From leveraging technology to create systems that work around family life to embracing the messy middle of entrepreneurial growth, Lindsay's journey proves that your creativity really can change lives—starting with your own. You'll want to read the full conversation and discover which of Lindsay's game-changing strategies align with your leadership style in our quiz.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Lindsay Buwalda

Lindsay Buwalda is the founder of Bliss Productions and BP Collective, a visionary event designer and branding expert known for crafting immersive weddings, corporate experiences, and transformational retreats. As a mother of two and creative powerhouse, she helps clients create meaningful brand experiences while navigating her own journey of entrepreneurship and leadership.

item.title: The Invisible Labor Behind Building a Pilates Empire: Paige Johnson's Story
item.content:encoded:
What if every woman building something has been conditioned to hide the hardest parts of what actually makes it work?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I met Paige through the fitness world, but what struck me most wasn't her incredible Pilates empire—it was how she talked about the invisible work that nobody sees. As someone who's built businesses while raising six kids, I knew we needed to have this conversation about what it really takes.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. The invisible labor of running a business extends far beyond what clients and customers see on the surface.
  2. Women in fitness and business spaces are often underestimated, but this can become a source of strength and motivation.
  3. Movement isn't just about physical fitness—it's a form of emotional and energetic leadership that impacts every area of life.
  4. Building community through shared experiences creates deeper connections than traditional business networking.
  5. Technology tools can help busy entrepreneurs stay organized and connected while managing multiple responsibilities.
  6. Finding your personal mantra helps you stay grounded when juggling business ownership and motherhood.
  7. Sometimes leadership means knowing when to rise up and when to let go with grace.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Movement isn't just fitness — it's emotional and energetic leadership"

The big idea

You know that moment when someone finally says the quiet part out loud? That's what happened when Paige Johnson and I talked about the invisible labor that goes into building anything meaningful. Here's a former gymnast who co-owns a thriving Pilates studio empire, and she's not interested in the glossy version of entrepreneurship. She wants to talk about what actually happens behind the scenes—the emotional labor, the constant decision-making, the way you hold space for others while building your own vision.

What fascinated me most was how Paige reframes everything we think we know about leadership in business. She's discovered that movement isn't just about getting stronger physically—it's about emotional and energetic leadership that ripples through every area of your life. When you understand your body as a tool for leadership, everything changes. You show up differently in boardrooms, in difficult conversations, in moments when you need to decide whether to push forward or let go with grace.

We talked about something that hits close to home for so many of us: being consistently underestimated. Whether you're in fitness, tech, or any industry where women are still fighting for credibility, there's this constant undercurrent of having to prove yourself. But here's what Paige has figured out—that underestimation can become fuel. It can sharpen your focus and clarify your mission in ways that easy acceptance never could.

The conversation took an unexpected turn when we discussed how building community through shared experiences creates deeper connections than any networking event ever could. Paige has created something beyond a business—she's built a space where women discover their own strength, literally and figuratively. And the technology tools and personal mantras she's developed to manage it all while raising a daughter? Pure gold for any of us juggling multiple responsibilities and trying to stay grounded in our own vision.

This episode will challenge how you think about the 'invisible' work you're already doing and help you see it as the leadership skill it actually is—plus there's a quiz that'll help you identify your own invisible labor patterns and how to leverage them.

item.title: Why Writing My Book Wasn't the Scary Part: A Lesson in Vulnerability
item.content:encoded:
What if the scariest part of writing a book isn't the writing—it's hitting publish?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I sat staring at my laptop screen for what felt like hours before I finally hit 'publish' on my book. All those months of pouring my heart onto pages suddenly felt easy compared to that one terrifying click. This episode is me getting real about why vulnerability feels manageable in private but absolutely terrifying when the world is watching.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Fear is often a signal that you're growing and moving in the right direction.
  2. Writing something vulnerable isn't as scary as sharing it with the world.
  3. Women hold entire worlds together before 9 AM and still question if they've done enough.
  4. Leading with vulnerability and beginner's mind creates authentic connection.
  5. Invisible labor of women deserves recognition and honor in leadership conversations.
  6. Taking the leap requires moving forward even when your hands are shaking.
  7. Perseverance through burnout and fear is part of the journey to authentic leadership.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"There are women holding entire worlds together before 9 AM, and still questioning if they've done enough."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you've created something deeply personal—maybe it's a presentation at work, a difficult conversation with your team, or even a social media post that matters to you—and your finger hovers over 'send'? That paralyzing moment when your heart pounds and you wonder if you're about to make a massive mistake? That's exactly where I found myself after months of writing what would become my book.

Here's what I discovered: the actual writing wasn't the scary part. Late nights crafting chapters, wrestling with words, digging deep into my own experiences as a CEO and mother—that felt safe somehow. It was just me and the page. But the moment I had to share those raw, honest stories with the world? Everything changed. Suddenly, I was about to expose the messy reality behind the polished exterior, the struggles that come with leading a nine-figure company while raising six kids, the invisible labor that happens before most people even wake up.

The truth is, we're conditioned to believe that vulnerability is weakness, especially as women in leadership. We think we need to have it all figured out before we can share our stories or step into bigger roles. But I've learned that fear isn't a stop sign—it's often the clearest indicator that you're about to do something that matters. When your hands are shaking and your heart is racing, that's not your cue to retreat. That's your signal that you're growing into the leader you're meant to be.

What struck me most during this reflection was how much we underestimate ourselves. You're orchestrating morning routines, managing teams, making decisions that affect people's lives, solving problems that would stump most consultants—and you're doing it all while questioning whether you've done enough. The skills you use to hold your world together aren't just valuable; they're the exact capabilities that make extraordinary leaders. But we've been taught to see them as ordinary, as just what we do.

This episode isn't just about my book journey—it's about every time you've held back because the stakes felt too high, every moment you've questioned whether your voice matters, every instance you've let fear keep you from sharing what could help others. Because here's what I know for sure: your story, your insights, your messy, beautiful journey through leadership and life—it all matters more than you realize.

item.title: How One Mom Used Entrepreneurship to Reclaim Her Power and Transform Her Family
item.content:encoded:
What if entrepreneurship isn't just about building a business—but about reclaiming your power as a woman and mother?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had one of those conversations with Kathleen Reed that left me thinking for days afterward. Here's a woman who didn't just start a business—she used entrepreneurship as a vehicle to completely transform how her family functions, sleeps, and thrives together.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Entrepreneurship can be a powerful tool for reclaiming personal agency and creating the life you want on your own terms.
  2. Orofacial myofunctional therapy addresses foundational issues that impact sleep, growth, and overall family wellness.
  3. Leadership doesn't require a fancy title—it happens in kitchens, carpool lines, and bedtime routines every day.
  4. Technology and AI can be leveraged to streamline work processes and create more time for meaningful family moments.
  5. Making invisible labor visible in both home and business settings is essential for sustainable success.
  6. Faith and family values can serve as powerful guiding principles in entrepreneurial ventures.
  7. Taking time for rest and adventure, like off-grid family experiences, is crucial for maintaining perspective and joy.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership doesn't require a fancy title—it happens in kitchens, carpool lines, and bedtime routines."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're managing everything for everyone else, but somehow your own power feels scattered to the wind? Kathleen Reed knows it intimately. As a mom who found herself drowning in invisible labor, she made a decision that changed everything: she became an entrepreneur not just to build a business, but to reclaim her agency and create the life her family actually needed.

What makes Kathleen's story so compelling isn't just that she built a successful practice in orofacial myofunctional therapy—it's how she approached the foundational issues that were impacting her family's sleep, growth, and overall wellness. She discovered that leadership doesn't require a corner office or a fancy title. The most important leadership happens in your kitchen, during carpool conversations, and in those quiet bedtime moments when you're shaping the humans who will change the world.

But here's where it gets really interesting: Kathleen figured out how to make invisible labor visible, both in her home and her business. She's leveraging technology and AI not to replace the meaningful work, but to streamline processes so she can create more space for what actually matters. This isn't about hustling harder—it's about working smarter so you can show up fully for the moments that count.

Throughout our conversation, Kathleen shared how her faith and family values became the guiding principles for every business decision. She's intentional about creating space for rest and adventure, including off-grid family experiences that help everyone maintain perspective and joy. It's a masterclass in building a life and business that actually serve each other, rather than competing for your attention.

If you've ever wondered whether you could use your entrepreneurial skills to solve the problems right in front of you—in your family, your community, your own life—this conversation will give you permission to start. Ready to discover how one mom's journey might unlock something powerful in your own story?

About Kathleen Reed

Kathleen Reed is the founder of West Coast Myofunctional Therapy, a specialized practice focused on orofacial myofunctional therapy for children and adults. As a mother of three and passionate advocate for whole-family wellness, she combines her expertise in health with her entrepreneurial spirit to help families achieve better sleep, growth, and vitality.

item.title: How Single Moms Can Rebuild Confidence and Find Balance After Burnout
item.content:encoded:
What if hitting rock bottom as a single mom was actually the launching pad for your biggest comeback?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had chills listening to Jessica Renee's story of complete burnout and her intentional journey back to herself. As someone who's navigated the chaos of motherhood while building a business, I know how easy it is to lose yourself in the process—and how hard it can be to find your way back.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. It's never too late to start rebuilding your life and confidence, even after experiencing complete burnout.
  2. Single motherhood doesn't disqualify you from pursuing entrepreneurial dreams and personal growth.
  3. Fueling yourself first isn't selfish—it's essential for showing up as your best self for your family.
  4. Small, intentional daily habits can create massive shifts in confidence and overall life balance.
  5. Using tools like AI can help streamline both work and home life, giving you precious time back.
  6. Rebuilding your identity after motherhood requires patience and intentional work, but it's absolutely possible.
  7. Wellness habits and mindset shifts are foundational to creating lasting change in your life.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You can rebuild, one small step at a time—it's never too late to start again."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you look in the mirror and barely recognize the woman staring back at you? Jessica Renee knows it intimately. She went from being completely lost in early motherhood—exhausted, burnt out, and feeling like she'd disappeared entirely—to intentionally rebuilding her confidence and creating a life that actually works for her and her family. This isn't about perfection or having it all together. It's about the messy, beautiful work of starting over.

What I love about Jessica's approach is how practical it is. She's not telling you to overhaul your entire life overnight. Instead, she's showing you how small, intentional daily habits can create massive shifts in your confidence and overall balance. We're talking about fueling yourself first—not because you're selfish, but because you literally cannot pour from an empty cup. Your kids, your work, your dreams—they all deserve the best version of you, and that requires you to invest in yourself.

Single motherhood comes with its own unique challenges, but Jessica proves it doesn't disqualify you from pursuing your entrepreneurial dreams or personal growth. In fact, it might just give you the clarity and determination you need to build something extraordinary. She shares how she's using tools like AI to streamline both work and home life, giving herself precious time back to focus on what matters most.

The truth is, rebuilding your identity after motherhood requires patience and intentional work, but it's absolutely possible. Jessica's story is proof that it's never too late to start over, one small step at a time. Her wellness habits and mindset shifts became the foundation for creating lasting change—not just for her, but for how she shows up for her family. If you're ready to stop feeling like you're drowning and start building the life you actually want, this conversation will give you the roadmap and the permission to begin.

About Jessica Renee

Jessica Renee is a solo mama, part-time hairstylist, Arbonne rep, and wellness advocate who is on a journey of rebuilding confidence and balance after experiencing burnout in the early years of motherhood. She's passionate about helping other moms reclaim their identity while balancing entrepreneurship and self-care.
