item.title: How to Build Multiple Businesses While Homeschooling: Heather Gerber's Blueprint
item.content:encoded:
What if everything you've been told about focus is wrong—and running multiple businesses actually makes you better at each?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to have Heather Gerber on because she's living proof that the 'pick one thing' advice we constantly hear is complete nonsense. While homeschooling her kids, she built not one but multiple thriving businesses, including Guiding Our Days, which serves thousands of families. Her approach completely flipped my thinking about what's actually possible.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Women can successfully run multiple businesses without having to choose one path over another.
  2. Community connection matters as much as curriculum when creating educational programs.
  3. Leading with service and heart creates more sustainable and meaningful businesses.
  4. Alternative education models can provide the flexibility modern families desperately need.
  5. Building values-driven businesses allows you to make impact while generating income.
  6. Entrepreneurship and motherhood can enhance each other rather than compete.
  7. Creating educational spaces that support both kids and parents fills a crucial market gap.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Women can build multiple businesses, nurture their families, and create meaningful community impact — all without having to choose one path over another"

The big idea

You've probably been told you need to choose: be a present mom OR build a business. Focus on one thing OR spread yourself too thin. Heather Gerber is here to tell you that's garbage advice. While homeschooling her children, she simultaneously ran a successful tanning business and built Guiding Our Days, an educational platform that now serves thousands of families worldwide.

What makes Heather's approach so brilliant isn't just that she's doing multiple things—it's how each part of her life actually strengthens the others. Her experience as a homeschooling mom didn't distract from her business building; it gave her the exact insights she needed to create something the market desperately wanted. She saw firsthand how isolated parents felt, how much they craved community connection alongside quality curriculum, and she built her business to solve those real problems.

The conversation gets really good when Heather breaks down how she thinks about service-driven entrepreneurship. Instead of starting with 'what can I sell,' she started with 'what do families actually need.' This heart-first approach didn't just create a more meaningful business—it created a more profitable one. When you're solving real problems for people you genuinely care about, the business model becomes so much clearer.

But here's what I love most about Heather's story: she's proving that alternative education models aren't just nice-to-have options for a few families. They're filling a crucial gap that traditional systems simply can't address. The flexibility, the community focus, the ability to adapt to each family's needs—these aren't luxuries. They're necessities for modern families trying to thrive, not just survive.

If you've ever felt like you're too scattered, too interested in multiple things, or too unwilling to put your family dreams on hold for business success, this conversation will give you a completely new framework for thinking about what's possible. Ready to discover how your 'too much' might actually be your superpower and take our quiz to find your own multi-business blueprint?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Heather Gerber

Heather Gerber is a serial entrepreneur, homeschooling mom, and founder of Guiding Our Days, an educational program supporting public charter independent study students. She successfully runs multiple businesses including her long-time tanning business while creating meaningful community impact through education.

item.title: Why Power Skills Are the Future of Leadership: AI-Powered Microlearning Insights
item.content:encoded:
What if the leadership skills you're desperately trying to learn in weekend workshops could be mastered in five-minute coffee breaks?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been fascinated lately by how we're all trying to squeeze leadership development into our already packed lives. When Ioanna Mantzouridou shared her research on AI-powered microlearning, it completely shifted how I think about skill building. This conversation will change how you approach your own growth.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Power skills like empathy, communication, and emotional intelligence are becoming more critical than technical skills in leadership roles.
  2. Microlearning allows busy professionals to develop essential skills in just 5-minute daily sessions, making growth sustainable and manageable.
  3. AI can effectively teach human skills by providing personalized feedback and adaptive learning experiences tailored to individual needs.
  4. Systems thinking and intuition are essential leadership capabilities that women often excel at naturally but need to recognize and develop intentionally.
  5. Scaling human connection in digital environments requires intentional strategies and tools that prioritize authentic relationship building.
  6. The invisible leadership work that women do daily—emotional labor, relationship maintenance, and team cohesion—deserves recognition and strategic development.
  7. Building confidence at work is a learnable skill that can be developed through consistent practice and the right learning frameworks.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The key to leadership isn't just strategy and hustle — but empathy, confidence, and connection."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you sign up for another leadership course, then watch the modules pile up in your inbox because who has three hours to dedicate to professional development? Ioanna Mantzouridou gets it, and she's pioneering something that actually works for how we live: AI-powered microlearning that develops your most crucial leadership skills in just five minutes a day.

Here's what caught my attention: the skills that matter most in leadership aren't the technical ones we spend thousands of dollars learning in MBA programs. They're power skills—empathy, emotional intelligence, authentic communication, and systems thinking. And here's the kicker: these are skills that many of us as women are already using daily, we just don't recognize them as leadership superpowers worth developing intentionally.

Think about it—when you're managing the emotional dynamics of a team meeting while tracking three different project timelines and intuiting that something's off with a key client relationship, you're demonstrating advanced leadership capabilities. But we rarely give ourselves credit for this invisible work, let alone systematically develop it. Ioanna's research shows that AI can actually be incredibly effective at teaching these human skills through personalized feedback and adaptive learning that meets you exactly where you are.

The microlearning piece is what makes this revolutionary. Instead of blocking out hours you don't have, you're building confidence, improving your communication, and strengthening your emotional intelligence in the same time it takes to scroll social media. The AI provides real-time feedback that's tailored to your specific challenges and learning style, making the development both efficient and sustainable.

What really struck me was Ioanna's insight about scaling human connection in our increasingly digital world. She's cracked the code on how to maintain authentic relationship building when you can't rely on in-person interactions. This isn't about replacing human connection—it's about amplifying your natural abilities and giving you frameworks to be more intentional about the leadership work you're already doing. Ready to discover how your daily 'invisible' leadership translates into measurable skills you can develop strategically?

About Ioanna Mantzouridou

Ioanna Mantzouridou is the founder of Dextego, a groundbreaking platform that uses AI-powered microlearning to help sales teams develop power skills like communication, negotiation, and emotional intelligence. She brings extensive global leadership experience to her entrepreneurial journey, focusing on reshaping the future of learning through innovative technology solutions.

item.title: From Invisible to Unstoppable: How Women Leaders Can Transform Burnout into Purpose
item.content:encoded:
What if every load of laundry you've folded is actually proof of your world-changing leadership abilities?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I recorded this episode at 5:47 AM after staying up until midnight coordinating three different family schedules, responding to urgent work emails, and somehow making sure everyone had what they needed for the next day. I realized I wasn't just 'managing'—I was leading, and it was time someone said that out loud.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Invisible labor performed by women is actually a form of world-shaping leadership that deserves recognition and honor.
  2. Burnout is not a personal failure but a cultural symptom of systems that don't value women's contributions.
  3. You can reclaim your purpose through small one-degree shifts rather than dramatic life overhauls.
  4. Domestic diplomacy, ritual creation, and sisterhood are revolutionary acts of leadership.
  5. You don't need credentials or permission to lead - you already have the spark within you.
  6. Presence matters more than five-year plans when it comes to creating meaningful change.
  7. Every woman has the power to turn pain into purpose and become a changemaker right where she is.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You don't need credentials to lead. You need love."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're holding everyone and everything together, but somehow you still feel invisible? When you're orchestrating entire worlds—managing calendars, smoothing over conflicts, anticipating needs before anyone even knows they have them—yet you wonder if you're actually making any real impact? I want to tell you something that might sound radical: what you're doing isn't just caretaking. It's world-shaping leadership, and it's time we called it what it is.

Here's what I've learned after building a nine-figure company while raising six kids: your burnout isn't a personal failing. It's a symptom of a culture that doesn't recognize the sophisticated leadership skills you use every single day. When you negotiate peace between fighting siblings, you're doing conflict resolution. When you create traditions that bring your family together, you're building culture. When you anticipate what your team needs before they ask, you're demonstrating emotional intelligence that Fortune 500 companies pay consultants millions to teach. The problem isn't that you're not a leader—it's that no one's been calling it leadership.

But recognition is just the beginning. The real transformation happens when you start making one-degree shifts—tiny pivots that honor your natural leadership style instead of fighting against it. You don't need to blow up your life or wait for permission to start leading differently. You already have everything you need. Your ability to hold complexity, to see the human side of every situation, to create connection where there was chaos—these aren't soft skills. They're superpowers disguised as ordinary moments.

I'm talking about domestic diplomacy as a transferable skill, ritual creation as revolutionary act, and sisterhood as a form of activism. I'm talking about presence over five-year plans, purpose over perfection, and the courage to trust that the spark you feel inside you matters. Because every woman who steps into her power creates permission for another woman to do the same. Every time you honor your invisible labor as real leadership, you're changing what's possible for all of us. Ready to discover exactly how your unique leadership style can transform not just your life, but everyone around you? Let's dig into the full conversation and take the quiz to identify your leadership superpower.

item.title: From Corporate Law to Fighting Human Trafficking: One Woman's Bold Leadership
item.content:encoded:
What happens when you walk away from million-dollar corporate law to go undercover rescuing trafficked children?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I sat across from Leigh Dundas knowing I was about to hear something extraordinary. Here's a woman who had everything by corporate standards—yet chose to risk it all for something bigger. Her story will challenge everything you think you know about what leadership actually costs.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. True leadership often requires making bold career pivots that align with your deeper values and purpose.
  2. Protecting human rights and freedoms requires both grassroots action and individual courage to stand up for what's right.
  3. The emotional toll of advocacy work demands intentional self-care strategies and strong support systems.
  4. Corporate skills can be powerfully redirected toward social justice and humanitarian causes.
  5. Medical freedom and civil liberties are fundamental human rights worth defending at every level.
  6. Undercover operations against trafficking require meticulous planning, courage, and collaboration with local authorities.
  7. Grassroots movements have the power to create meaningful change when individuals step up to lead.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership isn't about titles or corner offices—it's about having the courage to fight for what's right, even when the stakes are high."

The big idea

You know that voice inside your head that whispers about using your skills for something bigger? Leigh Dundas didn't just listen to it—she followed it straight into some of the most dangerous work on the planet. What started as a successful corporate litigation career became undercover missions to rescue children from trafficking rings. And the leadership lessons from her journey will stop you in your tracks.

Here's what struck me most about Leigh's story: she didn't abandon her corporate skills when she pivoted to human rights work. She weaponized them. The same meticulous planning that served her in high-stakes litigation now guides her through undercover operations. The same fearless advocacy she used for Fortune 500 clients now protects the most vulnerable among us. She's proof that your professional expertise isn't separate from your purpose—it's fuel for it.

But let's talk about what this kind of leadership actually costs. Leigh doesn't sugarcoat the emotional toll of advocacy work, especially when you're literally going undercover to fight child trafficking. She shares the self-care strategies that keep her grounded and the support systems that make this work sustainable. Because here's the truth: you can't pour from an empty cup, especially when the stakes are this high.

What fascinated me most was how Leigh bridges individual courage with grassroots action. She's not waiting for someone else to solve these problems—she's creating the movements that do. From medical freedom to civil liberties, she shows us how one person stepping up can create ripple effects that change everything. And the collaboration required? It's leadership at its most essential level.

This conversation will challenge you to think differently about career pivots, about using your voice, and about what true courage looks like when you're in the driver's seat. If you've ever wondered whether your corporate skills could translate to world-changing work, or if you're feeling called to something bigger but don't know where to start, this episode is your roadmap to discover just how powerful your leadership can be when it's aligned with your deepest values.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Leigh Dundas

Leigh Dundas is a human rights attorney, modern-day abolitionist, and award-winning public speaker who transitioned from representing Fortune 500 companies to leading undercover missions against child trafficking networks. She's become a powerful voice for civil liberties and medical freedom, advocating for human rights both globally and domestically.

item.title: From High School Coach to Tech CEO: How to Build a Company by Accident
item.content:encoded:
What if the best CEOs aren't made in boardrooms, but on high school basketball courts and dance studios?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I couldn't stop thinking about Alyssa's story long after we hung up. Here's a woman who literally stumbled into building a tech company while volunteering as a dance coach, and somehow that 'accident' taught her more about authentic leadership than any business school ever could. Her journey reminded me that our most unconventional experiences often become our greatest strengths.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Leadership skills can be developed in unexpected places, including volunteer coaching roles.
  2. The most successful businesses often solve problems you've personally experienced.
  3. Systems thinking and intuition are not opposites but complementary leadership tools.
  4. Invisible labor in motherhood translates directly to valuable leadership capabilities.
  5. Starting before you feel ready is often the key to breakthrough success.
  6. Humor and authenticity can be powerful tools for leading through uncertainty.
  7. Building a company accidentally can lead to more authentic and sustainable growth.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Invisible labor IS leadership and women need to own that truth"

The big idea

When Alyssa Nolte started volunteering as a high school dance coach, she had no idea she was getting a masterclass in CEO skills. Fast forward a few years, and she's running a tech company—not because she followed some prescribed entrepreneurial playbook, but because she learned to solve real problems with the same heart and grit she brought to coaching teenagers.

Here's what struck me most about Alyssa's approach: she didn't wait until she felt 'ready' to lead. Whether she was figuring out how to motivate a room full of teenage dancers or building systems for her growing company, she trusted her instincts and started before she had all the answers. That volunteer coaching role? It taught her everything about reading people, managing personalities, and creating accountability—skills that translate directly to the C-suite.

But here's the real game-changer in our conversation: Alyssa proved that systems thinking and intuition aren't opposing forces. As mothers, we're constantly doing invisible labor—anticipating needs, connecting dots, managing multiple moving pieces simultaneously. These aren't just 'soft skills'—they're the exact capabilities that make exceptional leaders. Alyssa built her company by recognizing that the problem-solving she was already doing in her daily life had real market value.

What I love about Alyssa's story is how she embraces the messiness of 'accidental' growth. She didn't have a five-year plan or venture capital backing. She had humor, authenticity, and a willingness to figure it out as she went. Her path proves that sustainable business growth often comes from staying true to who you are rather than trying to fit some predetermined mold of what a CEO should look like. Ready to discover which of these unconventional leadership lessons might be hiding in your own unexpected experiences?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Alyssa Nolte

Alyssa Nolte is the CEO of TruVue and host of two podcasts: The Growth Signal and Taking Back Monday. She's an accidental entrepreneur who built her tech company from the ground up, starting as a volunteer dance coach and evolving into a systems-thinking leader who believes intuition and strategy can coexist in business.

item.title: From Corporate Careers to Restroom Revolution: The UNICORN Story
item.description: Episode 26 | with Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan
item.content:encoded:
What if the business opportunity you're looking for is hiding in plain sight in every public restroom?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've always believed the best entrepreneurs are the ones who see problems the rest of us have learned to ignore. When I heard how Denielle and Thyme turned an everyday frustration into a Fortune 500 solution, I knew you needed to hear their story. These two cousins didn't just build a business—they built a movement around dignity and inclusion.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Leadership often starts with noticing what others overlook rather than inventing something completely new.
  2. Building a business with family requires clear boundaries and defined roles to maintain both relationships and success.
  3. Mission-driven companies can scale to Fortune 500 clients when they solve real problems with thoughtful solutions.
  4. Restroom equity is about dignity, wellness, and inclusion, not just convenience.
  5. Transforming invisible gaps into business opportunities requires courage to challenge the status quo.
  6. Co-CEO partnerships can work when both leaders bring complementary strengths and shared vision.
  7. Scaling a values-based company requires maintaining your core mission while adapting to larger market demands.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership isn't always about inventing something new — sometimes it's about noticing what's been ignored, and daring to redesign it."

The big idea

You know that moment when you walk into a public restroom and immediately start calculating your exit strategy? Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan not only noticed that universal experience—they decided to do something about it. These two cousins spotted what most of us have resigned ourselves to accepting: the complete lack of equity and dignity in public restroom experiences, especially for women.

What started as a simple observation turned into UNICORN, a company that's now working with Fortune 500 clients to revolutionize restroom access and product equity. But here's what I love most about their story—they didn't wait for permission to solve this problem. They saw an invisible gap that affected millions of people daily and had the courage to challenge an industry that had remained unchanged for decades.

Building a business with family brings its own unique challenges, and Denielle and Thyme share exactly how they've navigated the delicate balance of maintaining their relationship while scaling a mission-driven company. Their co-CEO partnership works because they brought complementary strengths to the table and never lost sight of their shared vision: that restroom equity isn't just about convenience—it's about basic human dignity, wellness, and inclusion.

The most powerful takeaway from our conversation? Leadership often starts with noticing what others overlook rather than trying to invent something completely new. Sometimes the biggest opportunities are hiding in the problems we've all learned to tolerate. Denielle and Thyme prove that when you combine courage with purpose, you can transform an everyday frustration into a business that creates real change. Ready to discover what invisible opportunities might be right in front of you?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan

Denielle Finkelstein and Thyme Sullivan are cousins and co-founders of UNICORN, a company revolutionizing restroom equity through innovative product dispensers. They left successful corporate careers to build a mission-driven business now serving Fortune 500 companies including Chase, American Express, and major airports across the country.

item.title: How to Build an Ethical Fashion Empire Without Burning Out
item.content:encoded:
What if your biggest burnout moment was actually the blueprint for building a business that doesn't drain you?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about what it really costs us to build something meaningful. When I met Kathryn Letson, founder of By The Sea Company, I was struck by how she turned her corporate fashion burnout into a thriving ethical empire that actually energizes her instead of depleting her.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Burnout can become the catalyst for creating a business model that truly fits your life and values.
  2. Ethical fashion means considering the entire supply chain, from materials to manufacturing to worker conditions.
  3. You don't have to sacrifice your wellbeing to build something significant in business.
  4. Starting small at farmers markets can be a powerful way to test and refine your business concept.
  5. Living in alignment with your environment can significantly impact your creativity and leadership style.
  6. Building a sustainable business requires designing systems that support both profit and principles.
  7. The future of fashion lies in innovative, eco-conscious textiles that don't compromise on style or function.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You don't have to sacrifice your wellbeing to build something big."

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about burnout: it's not just a warning sign—it can be the exact roadmap you need to build a business that actually fits your life. Kathryn Letson discovered this when her corporate fashion career left her completely drained, and she made the bold decision to start over with a completely different approach to both business and life.

What she built next will change how you think about scaling without sacrificing your soul. By The Sea Company now works with over 3,000 retailers, but here's the kicker—Kathryn designed every system to support her wellbeing first, profits second. She started at farmers markets, testing not just her products but her entire business philosophy: that you can build something significant without burning yourself out in the process.

The conversation gets really interesting when we dive into what 'ethical fashion' actually means beyond the buzzwords. Kathryn walks through her entire supply chain—from sourcing materials that don't harm the planet to ensuring fair worker conditions—and shows how these values became competitive advantages, not obstacles. She's proving that consumers are hungry for brands that align with their values, and they're willing to pay for it.

But what struck me most was how Kathryn's environment shapes her leadership. Living by the ocean isn't just a lifestyle choice—it's a business strategy that fuels her creativity and keeps her grounded in what matters. She's created a model that lets her be fully present for her business and her life, and the results speak for themselves.

If you're tired of the hustle-until-you-drop mentality and ready to build something sustainable in every sense of the word, this episode will give you permission to design your business around your values instead of someone else's definition of success—and our quiz will help you identify which of your core values should be driving your next business decision.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kathryn Letson

Kathryn Letson is the founder of By The Sea Company, an ethical fashion brand that has grown to serve over 3,000 retailers. After experiencing burnout in NYC's high-pressure fashion industry, she pivoted to create a sustainable business model rooted in ergonomic design and eco-conscious textiles from her base in Kauai.

item.title: Why Men Need to Step Up Emotionally at Home (Not Just Financially)
item.content:encoded:
What if your husband's 'helping' around the house is actually the problem holding your marriage back?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I'll be honest—I was nervous to have my first male guest on The Manage Her. But Bart Morse completely shifted how I think about partnership at home. His insights on why men need to move beyond financial providing into true emotional leadership? Game-changing for every woman drowning in the mental load.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. True masculine leadership at home goes far beyond providing financially—it requires emotional presence and shared responsibility for family systems.
  2. The 'nice guy' syndrome and people-pleasing behaviors actually create distance in relationships rather than the safety they're intended to provide.
  3. Boundaries are external limits while principles are internal values—understanding this distinction transforms how couples create safety together.
  4. Men can begin sharing the mental load by taking ownership of family calendars, systems, and forward-thinking planning.
  5. The '8 Mountains' framework provides a holistic approach to fulfillment that encompasses marriage, parenting, health, finances, and personal growth.
  6. Power struggles in marriage transform into partnership when both people commit to radical ownership of their roles and responsibilities.
  7. Shame is often the root barrier preventing men from stepping into deeper emotional leadership and authentic vulnerability.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"True leadership at home isn't just about providing and protecting—it's about emotionally showing up for your family every single day."

The big idea

Here's what I know to be true: you're exhausted from carrying the invisible weight of your family's life. The birthday parties to plan, the permission slips to remember, the emotional temperature of everyone in your house to monitor and manage. And while your partner might 'help' when you ask, that word itself—help—is part of the problem.

Bart Morse broke this down in a way that finally made sense to me. When men position themselves as helpers rather than owners of family systems, they're actually creating more work for us, not less. True partnership means he doesn't need your permission or direction to notice that your daughter needs new shoes or that the family calendar is falling apart. It means he's thinking three steps ahead about what the family needs, just like you do every single day.

But here's where it gets really interesting—Bart shared how shame often keeps men stuck in this helper role. They want to step up emotionally, but they don't know how, and instead of learning, they retreat into what feels safe: providing financially and doing tasks when asked. The problem is, this creates the very distance in marriage that nobody wants. You end up feeling like a manager rather than a partner, and he ends up feeling like he can never do anything right.

The solution isn't about lowering your standards or being grateful for bare minimum effort. It's about both people taking radical ownership of their roles. Bart's framework helps couples move from power struggles to true partnership, where the mental load is genuinely shared because both people are thinking systematically about the family's needs. This conversation will challenge everything you thought you knew about why your partnership feels so unbalanced—and give you a roadmap for the kind of marriage where you're truly in it together.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Bart Morse

Bart Morse is the founder of the Lion's Den Program, a transformational coaching program for men. He specializes in helping men move beyond traditional masculine roles of providing and protecting to become true emotional leaders in their relationships and families.

item.title: How to Parent with Purpose: Building Meaning and Resilience in Your Children
item.content:encoded:
What if protecting your kids from struggle is actually the worst thing you can do for their future?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've spent years wrestling with how to raise six kids who can handle whatever life throws at them. When I sat down with Pam Roy to discuss Viktor Frankl's approach to parenting, I realized I'd been thinking about resilience all wrong—and you might be too.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Parenting from the passenger seat means guiding your children without controlling their journey, allowing them to develop their own sense of purpose and direction.
  2. Meaning is more essential than happiness for building resilience and mental health in children.
  3. Discomfort and challenges are necessary for building resilience—protecting children from all difficulties actually weakens them.
  4. True belonging comes from authentic connection and shared values, not from fitting in or conforming to others' expectations.
  5. Teaching children to find meaning in their struggles helps them develop the mental tools to handle life's inevitable challenges.
  6. Community-based learning and mentorship provide more valuable real-world experience than traditional classroom settings alone.
  7. Traditions and rituals create powerful anchors of meaning and connection that help combat the loneliness epidemic affecting young people.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Raising resilient, purpose-driven kids begins with our own courage to live authentically."

The big idea

Here's what no one tells you about raising resilient kids: the secret isn't bubble-wrapping them from life's hard moments. It's teaching them to find meaning in those moments instead. Pam Roy brought Viktor Frankl's profound wisdom into our conversation, and it completely shifted how I think about guiding my children through an uncertain world.

The biggest revelation? You need to parent from the passenger seat, not the driver's seat. This means guiding your children without controlling their every move, allowing them to develop their own sense of purpose and direction. When you constantly rescue them from discomfort or make all their decisions, you're actually robbing them of the chance to build the mental muscles they'll need as adults. Think about it—every time you swoop in to solve their problems, you're sending the message that they can't handle life themselves.

Pam shared something that hit me right in the chest: meaning matters more than happiness when it comes to building resilience. We've become obsessed with making sure our kids are happy all the time, but what if we focused on helping them find purpose in their struggles instead? When children learn to extract meaning from difficult experiences, they develop an internal compass that guides them through future challenges. This isn't about making them suffer—it's about not shielding them from the growth that comes from working through problems.

The conversation also dove deep into how true belonging happens. It's not about fitting in or conforming to what everyone else is doing. Real connection comes from authentic relationships built on shared values and genuine understanding. This is why creating family traditions and rituals matters so much—they become anchors of meaning that combat the loneliness epidemic we're seeing in young people today. Community-based learning and mentorship also provide the real-world experience that traditional classroom settings simply can't match.

This episode will challenge everything you think you know about protecting your children and give you a completely new framework for raising kids who don't just survive—they thrive. Ready to discover which parenting approach you're actually using and whether it's building the resilience your kids will need?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Pam Roy

Pam Roy is a writer, speaker, and visionary thought leader who reimagines parenting, education, and purpose in our rapidly changing world. She is the author of Parenting from the Passenger Seat and Viktor E. Frankl: The Search for Meaning, a partner at the Viktor Frankl Institute of America, and an executive producer of films spotlighting the youth mental health crisis.

item.title: Why Self-Management is the Foundation of Great Leadership with Lynda Harvey
item.content:encoded:
What if the reason you can't lead others effectively is because you haven't learned to lead yourself first?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot lately about why some leaders seem to effortlessly command respect while others struggle to get their teams to follow through. After my conversation with former corporate executive Lynda Harvey, I realized the answer isn't about charisma or titles—it's about something much more fundamental.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Self-management is the foundational skill that must be mastered before you can effectively manage others.
  2. Mothers naturally develop many management skills through daily parenting that translate directly to workplace leadership.
  3. Knowing who you don't want to be as a leader is just as important as defining who you want to become.
  4. Tactical missions and small daily practices build leadership confidence more effectively than waiting for formal titles.
  5. Emotional intelligence and practical empathy are becoming essential leadership skills in the modern workplace.
  6. Building a personal brand around your expertise can create new career opportunities beyond traditional corporate paths.
  7. Self-awareness about your triggers and reactions allows you to lead from a place of calm rather than chaos.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership doesn't start in the boardroom—it starts in your own living room."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're in a meeting and the leader is clearly reactive, scattered, or just... off? Their energy ripples through the entire room, and suddenly everyone's on edge. Now flip that scenario: think about the leaders who walk into a room and instantly create calm confidence. The difference isn't their resume or their corner office—it's their level of self-management.

Lynda Harvey spent years climbing the corporate ladder before she realized this fundamental truth: you cannot effectively manage others until you've mastered managing yourself. This isn't just leadership theory—it's practical reality. When you're triggered, reactive, or operating from a place of chaos internally, your team feels it. They mirror it. And your leadership impact crumbles, no matter how brilliant your strategy might be.

Here's what I love about Lynda's approach: she recognizes that as women, especially as mothers, we're already developing these self-management skills daily. Every time you de-escalate a toddler meltdown while keeping your own emotions in check, you're practicing leadership. When you juggle seventeen different family logistics while staying calm and solution-focused, you're building management muscle. The problem isn't that we lack these skills—it's that we don't recognize their value or know how to translate them into workplace leadership.

The conversation gets really practical around building what Lynda calls 'tactical missions'—small daily practices that build your leadership confidence from the inside out. Instead of waiting for someone to hand you a title or promotion, you start leading yourself first. You become aware of your triggers. You practice responding instead of reacting. You build your personal brand around your expertise. These aren't soft skills—they're the foundational skills that separate good leaders from great ones.

What struck me most was Lynda's insight about knowing who you don't want to be as a leader being just as important as defining who you want to become. We've all worked for leaders who left us feeling drained, unheard, or undervalued. That's valuable data about the kind of leader you want to be—if you're willing to do the self-awareness work to make sure you don't repeat those patterns. Ready to discover which self-management skills you've already mastered and which ones need your attention?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Lynda Harvey

Lynda Harvey is a former corporate leader at Verizon and Nordstrom who has transformed into a viral TikTok educator and entrepreneur. She now runs a thriving micro-learning platform focused on social leadership while raising two teenagers and helping other leaders develop self-awareness and emotional intelligence.

item.title: Why Leadership Is a Behavior (Not a Title) + Credit Confidence for Women
item.content:encoded:
What if the most burned-out women in corporate America are actually the closest to unlocking generational wealth?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to bring Elizabeth Taylor on because she's cracking the code on something I see everywhere—brilliant women managers who are drowning in burnout while missing massive financial opportunities. This conversation shifted how I think about leadership behavior versus leadership titles, and why your credit confidence might be the secret weapon you didn't know you needed.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Leadership is fundamentally a behavior and mindset, not a position or title you hold.
  2. Women managers between 35-45 experience the highest rates of burnout and need specific strategies to combat this epidemic.
  3. Developing a clear leadership brand helps you show up consistently and confidently in your role.
  4. Credit confidence is a crucial but underrated leadership skill that directly impacts your freedom and opportunities.
  5. Being risk-aware rather than risk-averse allows women to make informed financial decisions with confidence.
  6. The current generational wealth transfer presents unprecedented opportunities for women who are financially prepared.
  7. Balancing ambition with rest and joy is essential for sustainable leadership success.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Leadership isn't a title — it's a behavior."

The big idea

Here's what no one tells you about leadership: it has absolutely nothing to do with the title on your business card. Elizabeth Taylor and I dove deep into why women managers—especially those of us juggling everything between 35 and 45—are burning out at unprecedented rates, and it's not just because we're working harder. It's because we're leading from a place of title rather than behavior, and that fundamental misunderstanding is costing us our sanity and our wealth.

The truth is, leadership is a behavior you choose every single day. It's how you show up in meetings, how you handle conflict, how you make decisions when no one's watching. When you start operating from this mindset instead of waiting for permission from your title, everything changes. Elizabeth breaks down exactly how to develop your leadership brand—that consistent, confident way you show up that people recognize and trust. This isn't about putting on a performance; it's about getting crystal clear on who you are as a leader so you can stop second-guessing yourself in every situation.

But here's where it gets really interesting—Elizabeth connects leadership behavior directly to financial confidence, and specifically something she calls 'credit confidence.' Most of us think about credit as this boring financial housekeeping task, but she reframes it as a leadership skill that directly impacts your freedom and opportunities. When you understand how to be risk-aware rather than risk-averse, you can make informed financial decisions that build generational wealth instead of playing it safe and staying stuck.

The timing couldn't be better. We're in the middle of the largest generational wealth transfer in history, and women who are financially prepared are positioned to capitalize on opportunities that our mothers and grandmothers never had access to. But preparation means more than just saving money—it means developing the leadership behaviors and financial confidence to recognize and seize those opportunities when they come. Elizabeth walks through exactly how to balance your ambition with the rest and joy that make leadership sustainable long-term, because burning out before you build wealth helps no one. Ready to discover your leadership behavior style and get your credit confidence score?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Elizabeth Taylor

Elizabeth Taylor, known as @thecorporatebestie, is a leadership trainer, sales coach, and organizational development expert who specializes in helping high-performing women transition into management roles without burning out. She combines her expertise in leadership development with her background in the credit industry to help women build both professional confidence and financial literacy.

item.title: From Homeschooling to Executive Coaching: Building Leaders at Home and Work
item.content:encoded:
What if raising kids on commitments instead of rules could revolutionize how you lead at work?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've always believed that motherhood is leadership training in disguise, but Amanda Jaggard takes this truth to a whole new level. Her journey from homeschooling mom to executive coach isn't just inspiring—it's a masterclass in recognizing the incredible skills we develop in our homes and how to leverage them in business.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Commitments create stronger foundations than rules because they require personal ownership and buy-in from everyone involved.
  2. Creating intentional culture at home through language, agreements, and respect directly translates to better leadership skills in business.
  3. The transition from homeschooling to entrepreneurship requires recognizing that your parenting experience has developed valuable business skills.
  4. Building a coaching practice successfully requires both mastering the art of coaching and developing strong business fundamentals.
  5. Preparing for empty nest season while your children are still home allows for a smoother transition and stronger family relationships.
  6. Single mothers can raise confident leaders by modeling resilience and teaching children to take ownership of their choices.
  7. Executive coaching principles applied to parenting create households where everyone feels respected and empowered to grow.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"We raised our boys on commitments instead of rules, and it changed everything about how our family operated."

The big idea

You know that feeling when someone puts words to something you've always felt but never quite articulated? That's exactly what happened when Amanda shared her approach to raising her sons on commitments rather than rules. Instead of saying 'you have to do this because I said so,' she created a family culture where everyone—including her kids—had skin in the game through mutual agreements and personal ownership.

Here's what blew my mind: Amanda realized that everything she was doing as a homeschooling mother was actually sophisticated leadership development. She was creating culture, facilitating difficult conversations, managing multiple personalities, and developing people—sound familiar? These aren't just parenting skills; they're executive-level competencies that most people pay thousands to learn in corporate training programs.

As a single mom, Amanda faced the unique challenge of preparing both herself and her children for her eventual empty nest season while they were still living at home. Her strategy was brilliant: she didn't wait until they left to start building her coaching practice. Instead, she began recognizing and articulating the business skills she'd been developing all along, then systematically built the business fundamentals she needed to turn those skills into a thriving executive coaching practice.

What struck me most was Amanda's insight about modeling resilience for her children. She didn't try to shield them from her business-building journey or pretend everything was easy. Instead, she let them witness her taking ownership of her choices, pivoting when things didn't work, and building something meaningful from scratch. Talk about raising future leaders who understand that challenges are opportunities, not roadblocks.

The parallels between creating intentional family culture and building strong business culture are undeniable, and Amanda's story proves that the transition from full-time parenting to entrepreneurship isn't about starting over—it's about finally getting paid for skills you've been mastering for years. Ready to discover which of Amanda's leadership principles could transform your own approach to building culture at home and work?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Amanda Jaggard

Amanda Jaggard is an Executive Coach at Novus Global and co-founder of the Meta Performance Institute. After earning her Master's in Leadership and Ministry and homeschooling her sons for nearly a decade, she transitioned to executive coaching in 2014. As a single mother, she has successfully raised leaders while building a thriving coaching practice that helps others develop both their coaching skills and business acumen.

item.title: From Corporate Executive to Entrepreneur: Lyndsay Dowd's Guide to Reinvention
item.content:encoded:
What if job security is actually the riskiest career strategy you could choose in 2024?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about this conversation with Lyndsay Dowd because her story challenges everything we've been taught about building a 'safe' career. After 23 years at IBM, she walked away from the golden handcuffs to start over as an entrepreneur. Her perspective on why career security trumps job security completely shifted how I think about risk.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Career security is more valuable than job security in today's rapidly changing workplace landscape.
  2. Personal branding is a leadership skill, not a luxury, and essential for professional growth at any level.
  3. Transforming company culture from the inside out requires courage and strategic action.
  4. Community and genuine connections are the real fuel for both happiness and professional success.
  5. Reinvention can happen at any age or career stage with the right mindset and support system.
  6. Building an irresistible workplace starts with ditching toxic leadership patterns and leading with heart.
  7. The transition from corporate executive to entrepreneur requires embracing uncertainty while leveraging existing skills.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The secret to lasting success isn't job security — it's career security"

The big idea

You know that nagging feeling that your current role might not exist in five years? Lyndsay Dowd felt it too—after two decades climbing the corporate ladder at IBM. But instead of ignoring it, she did something most of us are terrified to consider: she bet on herself and made the leap to entrepreneurship. What she discovered will change how you think about career planning forever.

Here's the truth bomb Lyndsay dropped: job security is an illusion, but career security? That's something you can actually control. Career security means building skills, relationships, and a reputation that travels with you no matter what happens to your company, your industry, or the economy. It means becoming so valuable and well-connected that opportunities find you, not the other way around. And yes, it requires you to think of personal branding not as self-promotion, but as a leadership skill that's non-negotiable in today's workplace.

What I love about Lyndsay's approach is how practical it is. She didn't just wake up one day and quit—she spent years building her network, developing her voice, and creating systems that would support her transition. She also talks about something we don't discuss enough: how to transform toxic workplace cultures from the inside out before you decide to leave. Sometimes the company you're trying to escape is actually the perfect testing ground for the leadership skills you'll need as an entrepreneur.

The conversation gets really interesting when we dive into community building and why authentic connections aren't just nice-to-have—they're business-critical. Lyndsay breaks down exactly how she leveraged relationships (without being gross about it) and why leading with heart isn't soft leadership, it's smart leadership. If you've ever wondered whether it's too late to reinvent yourself professionally, or if you're curious about what it really takes to build an irresistible workplace culture, this episode will give you the roadmap and the courage to take action.

Lyndsay Dowd is a globally recognized speaker, founder of Heartbeat for Hire, two-time author, and culture-shifting coach who helps companies transform toxic leadership and build irresistible workplaces. After 23 years at IBM, she made the bold leap from corporate executive to successful entrepreneur, proving that reinvention can happen at any age.

item.title: How Faith and Financial Freedom Create Purpose-Driven Leadership
item.content:encoded:
What if retiring at 28 wasn't about having millions, but about rewriting your entire relationship with money?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to sit with Spring's story for days after we recorded. Here's a woman who walked away from traditional success at 28—not because she was burned out, but because she understood something most of us are still learning: your worth determines your wealth, not the other way around.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Financial freedom isn't about the amount of money you have, but about your relationship with money and alignment with purpose.
  2. Walking in purpose over paycheck requires courage, but creates authentic prosperity and fulfillment.
  3. Partnership in marriage and business amplifies impact when both people are aligned with their calling.
  4. Your wealth begins with understanding your worth, not the other way around.
  5. Building generational wealth requires intentional planning and legacy-focused thinking.
  6. Women must rewrite their relationship with money to step into their full leadership potential.
  7. Faith-based decision making can lead to greater financial and personal freedom than traditional career paths.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Your wealth begins with your worth and your legacy begins today"

The big idea

Spring Eatmon's decision to retire herself at 28 wasn't reckless—it was revolutionary. While most of us are climbing corporate ladders, Spring was busy redefining what financial freedom actually means. And here's what she discovered: it has nothing to do with the number in your bank account and everything to do with your relationship with money itself.

In our conversation, Spring breaks down how she shifted from chasing paychecks to walking in purpose, and the results speak for themselves. She's built a legacy-focused business empire by aligning her financial decisions with her faith and values. But what struck me most wasn't her business success—it was her clarity about worth versus wealth. She understood that when you know your inherent value, money becomes a tool for impact rather than a measure of identity.

We dig deep into the partnership piece too, because Spring's story isn't just about individual success. She and her husband have created something powerful by both stepping into their callings simultaneously. It's a masterclass in how alignment in marriage can amplify your impact in ways that traditional career paths simply can't match. They're not just building wealth—they're building generational wealth with intentional, legacy-focused thinking.

What Spring teaches us is that faith-based decision making isn't about being impractical—it's about being strategic in ways the world doesn't understand. When you make choices from a place of purpose rather than fear, you create authentic prosperity that goes far beyond financial metrics. For women especially, this requires completely rewriting our relationship with money and stepping into our full leadership potential. Spring's journey proves that sometimes the most radical thing you can do is trust your calling over conventional wisdom. Ready to discover what your own relationship with money is costing you and take our quiz to find out where you stand?

item.title: Why Your Mitochondria Are the Secret to Unstoppable Energy and Longevity
item.content:encoded:
What if your exhaustion isn't about your schedule, but about your cells literally running out of fuel?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I used to think being tired all the time was just part of being a high-achieving woman with six kids and a nine-figure company. Then I learned about mitochondria—and realized I'd been managing everything except the actual operating system of my body. This conversation with Kristina Kristen changed how I think about energy entirely.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Mitochondria function as your body's operating system, not just powerhouses, controlling energy, hormones, and aging processes.
  2. The NESTS Framework—Nutrition, Exercise, Sleep, Toxin avoidance, and Stress control—provides a systematic approach to mitochondrial health.
  3. Maternal mitochondrial DNA creates a unique health legacy that passes from mothers to children.
  4. Women can apply the same strategic thinking they use in business to optimize their cellular health and energy levels.
  5. Simple daily habits can dramatically improve mitochondrial function, leading to better fat burning, mental clarity, and longevity.
  6. Toxin avoidance and stress management are equally important as nutrition and exercise for cellular health.
  7. Resetting mitochondria requires a 28-day commitment but delivers lasting improvements in energy and vitality.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Your mitochondria are your body's operating system—not just the powerhouses"

The big idea

You know that feeling when your phone battery hits 20% and suddenly everything runs slower? That's exactly what's happening in your body when your mitochondria aren't functioning optimally. These cellular powerhouses aren't just energy producers—they're your body's entire operating system, controlling everything from how you burn fat to how your hormones function to how quickly you age.

Here's what blew my mind: as women, we carry a unique responsibility and opportunity. Our mitochondrial DNA passes directly to our children, meaning the health choices we make today literally shape the cellular legacy we leave behind. It's like being the CEO of not just our own energy, but our family's biological inheritance. And just like in business, we can apply strategic thinking to optimize this system.

Kristina introduced me to the NESTS Framework—Nutrition, Exercise, Sleep, Toxin avoidance, and Stress control—and it's as systematic as any business framework I've used. The key insight? We often focus on nutrition and exercise while completely ignoring toxins and stress, but all five elements are equally critical. Your mitochondria don't care how clean you eat if you're chronically stressed or exposed to environmental toxins that are literally poisoning your cellular engines.

The most encouraging part? You can reset your mitochondrial function in just 28 days with consistent daily habits. We're talking about simple changes that can dramatically improve your fat burning, mental clarity, and long-term vitality. Think of it as debugging your body's code—once you understand the system, you can optimize it with the same strategic precision you bring to everything else in your life. Ready to discover exactly how to implement the NESTS Framework and take the assessment that reveals your current mitochondrial health score?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kristina Kristen

Kristina Kristen is a lifestyle medicine educator, chemistry professor, and author of 10,000 Suns: The 28-Day Mitochondria Reset. She specializes in helping people optimize their cellular health through her NESTS Framework, combining her scientific background with practical wellness strategies.

item.title: When Motherhood Becomes Advocacy: Eden Marie James on Medical Freedom and Change
item.content:encoded:
What if the most powerful leadership training you'll ever receive comes from fighting for your child's life?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've met countless women who discovered their voice through necessity, but Eden Marie James's story stopped me in my tracks. When her child was injured, she didn't just seek answers—she became the solution, transforming personal devastation into a movement that's changing lives across the globe.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Personal pain can become your most powerful platform for creating change in the world.
  2. Motherhood often reveals leadership capabilities you never knew you had.
  3. Medical freedom and parental rights require active advocacy from informed mothers.
  4. Grassroots movements led by passionate mothers can drive significant policy change.
  5. Storytelling is one of the most essential tools in advocacy and leadership.
  6. Global issues like child trafficking require local leaders willing to take action.
  7. One woman's courage to speak up can inspire an entire movement of change.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Eden's response wasn't silence — it was action."

The big idea

You know that moment when everything you thought you knew gets turned upside down? That's exactly what happened to Eden Marie James when her child suffered a vaccine injury. But here's what makes Eden's story extraordinary—she didn't let that moment break her. Instead, she let it build her into the leader she was meant to become.

What struck me most about our conversation wasn't just Eden's courage to speak up when it would have been easier to stay quiet. It was how she channeled her pain into purpose with such strategic precision. She didn't just get angry—she got certified as a nutritionist. She didn't just complain—she founded a grassroots coalition. She turned her personal nightmare into a platform that's now advocating for medical freedom and parental rights on a scale she never imagined possible.

This is what real leadership looks like, and it's something I see happening everywhere: motherhood revealing capabilities in women that they never knew they possessed. Eden's journey from devastated mom to movement leader illustrates something I've witnessed time and again—when you're fighting for something bigger than yourself, you discover strength you didn't know existed. The skills you use to research treatments, coordinate care, and advocate for your child? Those same skills can drive policy change, build communities, and inspire other women to find their own voices.

What I love about Eden's approach is how she understands that storytelling isn't just sharing—it's leading. Every time she tells her story, she's giving other mothers permission to trust their instincts, question what they're told, and demand better for their families. She's proving that you don't need a corner office or a fancy title to create massive change. You just need to be willing to lead from exactly where you are.

Eden's work extends far beyond medical freedom—she's tackling issues like child trafficking and showing us that global problems require local leaders willing to take action. Her story will challenge you to look at your own pain points and ask yourself: What if this isn't just something that happened to me, but something that's preparing me to lead? If you're ready to discover how your personal struggles might be your greatest leadership training ground, you'll want to dive into the full conversation and take our leadership assessment to see where your advocacy journey might begin.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Eden Marie James

Eden Marie James is a certified clinical nutritionist, mother, and passionate advocate who founded the MAHA Mom Coalition after her child experienced a vaccine injury. She has spent nearly a decade working globally to combat child trafficking and is a fierce voice for medical freedom and parental rights.

item.content:encoded:
What if your biggest struggle is actually your greatest business asset?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, Hey friend, I had the most incredible conversation with Gabrielle Scout Maio about how her bipolar diagnosis became the foundation for building a million-dollar PR empire. This episode completely reframes what we think we know about success, leadership, and turning our pain into power.

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Mental health challenges can become entrepreneurial superpowers when properly channeled and understood.
  2. Emotional intelligence is more valuable than traditional hustle culture in building sustainable business success.
  3. Being early to innovation is difficult but creates lasting competitive advantages in business.

  5. Authentic leadership that embraces vulnerability creates stronger teams and better business outcomes.

The big idea

You know that thing you think disqualifies you from success? The struggle you've been hiding, the challenge you think makes you "too much" or "not enough" for big dreams? Gabrielle Scout Maio is here to flip that script entirely. She took her bipolar diagnosis and turned it into the cornerstone of a thriving PR empire, proving that emotional intelligence will beat hustle culture every single time.

What I love about Gabrielle's story is how she refused to see her mental health journey as a limitation. Instead, she recognized that her deep understanding of emotions, cycles, and human complexity gave her superpowers in the PR world. While others were grinding themselves into the ground, she was building sustainable success by honoring her natural rhythms and leveraging her emotional intelligence. She shows us that being early to innovation is hard, but it creates lasting competitive advantages that no amount of traditional hustle can match.

We dive deep into how motherhood didn't slow her down—it actually accelerated her business performance by forcing efficiency and crystal-clear priorities. Gabrielle's approach to leadership embraces vulnerability as strength, creating stronger teams and better business outcomes. She's living proof that women leaders naturally expand possibilities for everyone around them through their authentic leadership style.

This conversation will challenge everything you think you know about what makes a successful entrepreneur. Gabrielle reminds us that your pain has purpose and your story has power when you channel it into meaningful work. Get ready to see your own perceived "weaknesses" in a completely new light.

item.title: How One Mom With Brain Injury Fought CA Laws Denying 50K Kids School Access
item.content:encoded:
What if your biggest personal setback became the superpower you needed to change 50,000 lives?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I met Amy Bohn and immediately knew her story would hit you differently. Here's a woman who literally had to relearn how to read after a brain injury, yet somehow built a 30,000-member advocacy army that protected thousands of California kids from losing their education. Her journey from personal struggle to systemic change will make you rethink what's possible when you're fighting for something bigger than yourself.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Recovery from personal challenges can fuel your capacity to fight for others who need a voice.
  2. Organized parent advocacy can successfully defeat restrictive legislation even in hostile political environments.
  3. Understanding the systematic nature of legislative strategies helps you recognize patterns before they impact your family.
  4. Building community around shared values creates exponential impact beyond what any individual can achieve alone.
  5. Speaking up for vulnerable populations often requires stepping outside your comfort zone and into unfamiliar territory.
  6. Legislative changes in one state often serve as testing grounds for nationwide implementation.
  7. Effective advocacy requires both emotional passion and strategic understanding of political processes.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"What started as one exhausted mom showing up at the Capitol became a movement."

The big idea

You know that moment when you realize your personal pain might actually be preparing you for something much bigger? That's exactly what happened to Amy Bohn. After suffering a traumatic brain injury that left her unable to read aloud, she was rebuilding her own life when she stumbled across something that made her blood boil: California was about to deny 50,000 kids access to school through restrictive legislation. Instead of thinking 'someone should do something about this,' Amy became that someone.

What Amy did next will completely shift how you think about your own capacity to create change. Despite still recovering from her injury, she built a grassroots movement from scratch that grew to 30,000 members. She learned to navigate hostile political environments, decode legislative language, and turn parental frustration into organized action. The result? She didn't just stop bad legislation – she created a blueprint for how determined parents can take on entire systems and win.

Here's what struck me most about Amy's approach: she understood that this wasn't just about California. Legislative changes in one state become testing grounds for nationwide rollouts. By stopping harmful policies at the source, she was protecting families everywhere. Amy proves that you don't need political experience or perfect circumstances to become a force for change – you just need to care enough about something to step outside your comfort zone and learn as you go.

This conversation will show you how recovery from your own challenges can actually fuel your ability to fight for others who need a voice. Amy's story demonstrates that when you combine emotional passion with strategic thinking, you can achieve impact that goes far beyond what any individual should be able to accomplish alone. If you've ever wondered whether your voice really matters in the bigger picture, Amy's journey will give you your answer – and probably inspire you to use it.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Amy Bohn

Amy Bohn is the President and Co-Founder of PERK (Protection for the Educational Rights of Kids), one of California's most prominent advocates for parental rights and medical freedom. After recovering from a severe brain injury in 2018, she built a grassroots movement of over 30,000 members that has successfully challenged restrictive legislation affecting children's access to education.

item.title: Dr. Holly Wood: Why Pleasure Is Self-Care for Moms, Not Selfishness
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that prioritizing your pleasure isn't selfish—it's the self-care you've been missing?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I'll be honest—this conversation with Dr. Holly Wood made me rethink everything I thought I knew about pleasure and motherhood. As someone juggling CEO life with six kids, I've definitely felt that familiar tug between taking care of everyone else and honoring my own needs. This episode gave me language for something I've been feeling but couldn't quite articulate.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Pleasure is not selfish; it's a vital form of self-care that women deserve to prioritize in their lives.
  2. Motherhood naturally changes your relationship with your body and desire, and accepting this evolution is part of the journey.
  3. Open communication with your partner about your needs and desires is essential for maintaining intimacy through life's seasons.
  4. Understanding that women's desire works differently than men's can help normalize your experience and reduce shame.
  5. Creating safe spaces for pleasure in your relationship requires intentional effort from both partners.
  6. Dismantling shame around sexuality is an ongoing process that benefits your overall well-being.
  7. You have the right to pursue the sex and pleasure you desire, regardless of your life stage or circumstances.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Pleasure isn't selfish. It's self-care."

The big idea

You know that voice in your head that whispers you're being selfish when you think about your own pleasure? Dr. Holly Wood is here to tell that voice to take a seat. In this episode, we dive deep into why pleasure isn't just something nice to have—it's essential self-care that you have every right to prioritize, even as a busy mom.

Here's what I love about Dr. Wood's approach: she doesn't sugarcoat the reality that motherhood changes everything, including your relationship with your body and desire. Instead of pretending you can just bounce back to who you were before, she gives you permission to embrace this evolution and discover what pleasure looks like in this new season of your life. We talk about how women's desire works differently than men's—and why understanding this can free you from so much unnecessary shame and self-judgment.

The conversation gets real about communication too. Dr. Wood shares practical ways to talk with your partner about your needs and desires, even when (especially when) life feels overwhelming. She explains how creating safe spaces for intimacy requires intentional effort from both partners, and why dismantling the shame around sexuality is work that benefits every area of your life, not just your bedroom.

What struck me most was her clear message: you have the right to pursue the sex and pleasure you desire, regardless of your life stage or circumstances. This isn't about adding another item to your to-do list—it's about recognizing that your pleasure matters, your desires are valid, and taking care of this part of yourself actually makes you better at everything else you do. Ready to challenge everything you've been told about pleasure and discover what's possible when you give yourself permission to want what you want?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Holly Wood

Dr. Holly Wood is a sexologist, sex therapist, and women's sexual wellness advocate dedicated to helping women reclaim their sexuality and dismantle shame. She specializes in helping women prioritize pleasure as part of a healthy, fulfilling life.
