item.title: From Burnout to Breakthrough: Chef Lindsay Smith's Journey of Resilience
item.content:encoded:
What if running the same business for 19 years while raising kids and surviving divorce actually makes you a leadership genius?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I met Chef Lindsay Smith and immediately knew we had to talk. Here's a woman who's run the same restaurant for nearly two decades, raised children, navigated divorce, and completely reinvented herself—all while staying true to her vision. Her story isn't just about resilience; it's about recognizing when your business needs to evolve with you.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Your business should evolve as you evolve—pivot when life demands it rather than forcing yourself to fit an outdated model.
  2. Relationships are the foundation of long-term business success, often more valuable than formal education or credentials.
  3. Learning to delegate and shift from operator to owner creates space for both growth and personal freedom.
  4. Building authentic community in the digital age requires intentional in-real-life connections and vulnerability.
  5. Major life transitions like divorce can become catalysts for business reinvention and personal empowerment.
  6. Creativity often emerges from necessity and constraint, leading to breakthrough innovations like signature menu items.
  7. Middle age and perimenopause can bring unexpected clarity, confidence, and permission to choose differently.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"I realized at my 40th birthday that I couldn't breathe—and I had to make a change"

The big idea

When Chef Lindsay Smith started her restaurant 19 years ago, she had no idea she'd spend the next two decades learning some of the most valuable leadership lessons of her life—not in boardrooms, but in the trenches of daily operations, motherhood, and personal reinvention. Her journey reveals something most of us know but rarely talk about: your business needs to grow and change as you do, or you'll find yourself trapped in a model that no longer serves your life.

Lindsay's story hits on something so many of us experience—that moment when you realize you've been forcing yourself to fit into a business structure that worked for who you were five or ten years ago, but doesn't work for who you're becoming. Whether it was navigating divorce while keeping her restaurant running, or recognizing that her approach to leadership needed to shift as her children grew, Lindsay consistently chose evolution over stagnation. She learned to delegate not because she had to, but because stepping back from day-to-day operations created space for both business growth and personal freedom.

What struck me most about our conversation was how Lindsay built her success on relationships rather than credentials. In an industry where formal culinary training often defines credibility, she proved that authentic connections with customers, staff, and community matter more than any degree. Her signature menu items didn't come from textbooks—they emerged from creativity born of necessity, the kind of innovation that happens when you're solving real problems for real people.

The timing of Lindsay's biggest reinvention wasn't coincidental—it happened during her forties, in the midst of perimenopause and major life transitions. There's something powerful about this phase of life that gives us permission to choose differently, to stop apologizing for our needs, and to build businesses that actually support our lives instead of consuming them. Lindsay's willingness to be vulnerable and create authentic community, especially in our increasingly digital world, shows us what's possible when we lead with our whole selves. If you're ready to discover which phase of business evolution you're in and what your next breakthrough might look like, dive into the full conversation and take our leadership assessment.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Lindsay Smith

Lindsay Smith is the owner and executive chef of Nirvana by Chef Lindsay, a beloved farm-to-table restaurant in Laguna Beach that she's operated for 19 years. She started her culinary journey at 15 and became a restaurant owner by 26, building not just a business but a community through her monthly ladies' dinner series and commitment to authentic connection.

item.title: Why Money is Freedom: Essential Wealth Strategies Every Woman Needs to Know
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that 80% of women will die in poverty—but it's completely preventable?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to bring financial strategist Melissa Northway on the show because the statistics around women and money genuinely kept me up at night. As someone who's built a nine-figure company, I know firsthand that money isn't about greed—it's about freedom and choices.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Money equals freedom—the freedom to live life without financial worry and make choices based on values, not limitations.
  2. Women need to take control of their financial planning instead of leaving it to their partners, as statistics show elderly women are disproportionately poor.
  3. Your greatest asset is yourself—protect your earning potential with proper insurance and living benefit riders that provide access to death benefits while alive.
  4. Cash value life insurance functions as a Tier 1 asset with a zero floor, meaning you can't lose money while building tax-advantaged wealth.
  5. Asset swap strategies used by billionaires like Zuckerberg can be adapted for everyday women to optimize tax efficiency.
  6. Developing AI skills can increase income by 10-25%, making it crucial for women to embrace technology for financial advancement.
  7. Approaching money with a 'beginner's mind' and being coachable allows you to learn strategies that can transform your financial future.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Money is freedom—freedom to live the life you want without financial worry"

The big idea

Here's what no one talks about: money isn't just numbers on a spreadsheet. It's freedom. The freedom to make decisions based on your values instead of your bank account balance. The freedom to leave a bad situation, support causes you believe in, or simply sleep soundly at night without financial anxiety gnawing at you.

In this conversation with financial strategist Melissa Northway, we dive deep into why women disproportionately end up poor in retirement—and more importantly, what you can do about it starting today. Melissa pulls back the curtain on wealth-building strategies that have traditionally been reserved for high-net-worth individuals, breaking them down into actionable steps that any woman can implement. We're talking about the same asset swap strategies that billionaires like Zuckerberg use, adapted for real women building real wealth.

One of the biggest mindset shifts Melissa shares is recognizing that you are your greatest asset. This means protecting your earning potential becomes just as important as any investment strategy. We explore how cash value life insurance functions as what she calls a 'Tier 1 asset'—something with a zero floor where you literally cannot lose money while building tax-advantaged wealth. It's fascinating how the financial industry has overcomplicated concepts that, at their core, are designed to work for you.

What really struck me was Melissa's point about approaching money with a beginner's mind. Too many of us let past financial mistakes or lack of formal education keep us from learning strategies that could completely transform our futures. She even breaks down how developing AI skills can increase your income by 10-25%—proof that embracing technology isn't just smart, it's financially essential.

The bottom line? Taking control of your financial planning isn't optional anymore. It's survival. Whether you're just starting out or you've been building wealth for years, this episode will challenge how you think about money as a tool for creating the life you actually want. Ready to discover which wealth-building personality type you are and get your personalized action plan?

item.title: Why Toxic Resilience Is Keeping You Stuck (And What to Do Instead)
item.content:encoded:
What if the resilience you've been praised for your entire career is actually the thing keeping you trapped?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to pause this conversation with Dr. Katie Pritchett three times because she kept saying things that made me question everything I thought I knew about strength. As someone who's built a nine-figure company while raising six kids, I thought I had resilience figured out—turns out I'd been confusing survival with success.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Toxic resilience means wearing burnout as a badge of honor instead of recognizing it as a signal for change.
  2. Regeneration is more powerful than resilience because it allows things to break so you can build something better.
  3. Leadership is neuroscience—you need to understand how your people are wired to lead them effectively.
  4. Women must be involved in creating AI technology because only we can automate the invisible labor we see and do.
  5. Your childhood survival strategies may be running your adult leadership in ways that no longer serve you.
  6. Breaking generational cycles means choosing to inherit healing rather than pain for the next generation.
  7. Real transformation happens through experiential learning, not just consuming more information.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"I don't want him to inherit my pain—I want him to inherit my healing"

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about that resilience you've been so proud of: it might be toxic. Dr. Katie Pritchett dropped this truth bomb early in our conversation, and it changed how I think about everything from my leadership style to the example I'm setting for my daughters. Toxic resilience is when we wear burnout like a badge of honor instead of recognizing it as our body's urgent signal that something needs to change. Sound familiar?

Katie introduced me to a concept that's honestly revolutionary: regeneration over resilience. While resilience tries to bounce back to what was, regeneration allows things to break so you can build something better. Think about it—when you're constantly bouncing back to a system that's burning you out, you're just perpetuating the problem. Regeneration asks: what if we let this old way die so something healthier can grow?

We dove deep into how your childhood survival strategies might be running your adult leadership in ways that no longer serve you. That perfectionism that kept you safe as a kid? It's probably suffocating your team. That hypervigilance that helped you navigate family chaos? It's likely creating anxiety in your organization. Katie explained how leadership is fundamentally neuroscience—you need to understand how your people are wired to lead them effectively, and that starts with understanding your own wiring.

One of the most powerful moments in our conversation was when Katie talked about breaking generational cycles. As mothers and leaders, we have the choice to inherit healing rather than pain for the next generation. This isn't just about our children—it's about every person we lead, every system we touch, every legacy we create. Katie also made a compelling case for why women must be involved in creating AI technology, because only we can automate the invisible labor we see and do.

The real kicker? Katie reminded me that transformation happens through experiential learning, not just consuming more information. You can listen to every leadership podcast and read every business book, but until you actually experience doing things differently, nothing changes. Ready to move beyond toxic resilience and discover what regenerative leadership looks like in your life?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Katie Pritchett

Dr. Katie Pritchett is the founder of KPI Coaching, a leadership development and organizational consulting firm. With a PhD in experiential learning from UT Austin and years coaching Fortune 500 executives, she specializes in helping people and companies grow from the inside out through understanding the neuroscience of leadership.

item.title: The Money Story That's Costing Women Millions (And How to Rewrite It)
item.content:encoded:
By age seven, you'd already developed the money story that's costing you millions—and you don't even know it.

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I used to think my financial decisions were purely logical—spreadsheets, data, business sense. Then Stephanie Guttman walked me through how my seven-year-old self was still running my money choices, and everything clicked. This conversation will change how you think about wealth forever.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Your money story is formed by age 7 through interactions with parents, peers, and trusted adults, creating invisible scripts that drive your financial behavior.
  2. The four core money stories are avoidance, money worship, status, and vigilance—with vigilance being the only one correlated with positive financial outcomes.
  3. The investing gap costs women far more long-term than the wage gap because compound interest doubles your money roughly every 10 years.
  4. ETFs allow you to invest in hundreds of stocks with a single purchase, making investing accessible without needing to beat the market.
  5. Teaching children about investing before age 16 triples their likelihood of becoming confident investors as adults.
  6. Women receive only 2-3% of all available capital, making it crucial for women to start investing in women-led businesses and startups.
  7. Financial agency begins with teaching children to make real money decisions, not just theoretical lessons about saving.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The investing gap is costing women far more than the wage gap, and the only way to close it is to stop waiting and start investing."

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about money: your relationship with it was decided before you could tie your shoes. By age seven, through watching your parents argue about bills or celebrate a bonus, through absorbing the energy around financial conversations, you developed an invisible script that's been driving every financial decision you've made since. And for most of us, that script is costing us more than the wage gap ever could.

Stephanie Guttman breaks down the four core money stories we carry—avoidance, money worship, status, and vigilance. Here's the kicker: only one of these (vigilance) actually correlates with positive financial outcomes. The rest? They're keeping you small, scared, or spending in ways that sabotage your future wealth. But here's what really got my attention: the investing gap is destroying women's financial futures far more than unequal paychecks. When compound interest doubles your money roughly every 10 years, sitting on the sidelines isn't just cautious—it's costly.

The solution isn't complicated, but it does require rewriting those early scripts. Stephanie walks through how ETFs make investing accessible without needing to become a stock market genius, and why teaching our kids about real money decisions (not just piggy bank lectures) before age sixteen triples their likelihood of becoming confident investors. But this goes beyond personal wealth—women receive only 2-3% of all available capital, which means we need to start putting our money where our values are and invest in women-led businesses.

This isn't about becoming obsessed with money or turning into someone you're not. It's about recognizing that financial agency is freedom—the freedom to make choices aligned with your values, to support causes you believe in, and to build the life you actually want. Ready to discover which money story has been running your financial life and learn the simple steps to rewrite it for good?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Stephanie Guttman

Stephanie Guttman is the founder of Huntress Wealth, a financial wellness and planning firm dedicated to helping women reclaim confidence with their finances. She hosts the Talk Rich to Me podcast and authors The Arrow, a weekly newsletter helping women align their money with their values.

item.title: 3 Steps to Transform Your Family's Health Without Overwhelm (From a Mom Expert)
item.content:encoded:
What if your phone could actually heal your family instead of slowly destroying your biofield?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to have Julie E. Health on the show because as a mom of six, I'm always looking for ways to manage our family's health that don't add to my overwhelm. Her approach treats health management like the strategic leadership skill it actually is—and her insights about how our bodies reveal root causes completely shifted how I think about symptoms.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Your phone is destroying your biofield, but a simple Q-disc sticker can turn it into a healing tool.
  2. The 5-hour head-on-pillow rule rejuvenates glands that regulate all your hormones and can transform perimenopausal symptoms.
  3. Kids with bruises on shins and clumsiness often have weak kidneys affecting their equilibrium.
  4. Waking at specific times reveals which detox organs need support - you're not just waking to pee.
  5. C-section scars can cause back, neck, and shoulder pain years later by disrupting energy meridians.
  6. When your biofield is intact, your body reveals true root causes instead of giving false symptoms.
  7. Fevers in children often indicate toxins in the intestines, and clearing the gut can break the fever quickly.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"When the biofield is intact, healing is limitless."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're managing everything for everyone else's health, but somehow your own energy feels completely depleted? Julie E. Health gets it, and she's developed a three-step framework that transforms how you approach your family's wellness—without adding more chaos to your already full plate.

Here's what blew my mind: that phone you're probably reading this on right now? It's actively disrupting your biofield, but with a simple Q-disc sticker, you can actually turn it into a healing tool. Julie breaks down how our modern world is constantly attacking our energy systems, but there are strategic, simple shifts that protect and restore our natural healing capacity. Think of it like building the infrastructure your body needs to function optimally.

The conversation gets really practical when Julie explains her 5-hour head-on-pillow rule—this isn't just about sleep, it's about giving your glands the time they need to regulate hormones. For those of us navigating perimenopause while running businesses and families, this one insight could be a complete game-changer. She also reveals why your kids' clumsiness might actually indicate weak kidneys affecting their equilibrium, and how those mysterious 3 AM wake-ups are your body's way of telling you exactly which detox organs need support.

What I love about Julie's approach is how she connects seemingly unrelated symptoms to their true root causes. That C-section scar causing your shoulder pain years later? Those recurring fevers in your kids that seem to come out of nowhere? She explains how everything in our bodies is connected through energy meridians, and when we understand these connections, we can address the real issues instead of just managing symptoms. Julie's framework gives you the tools to read your family's bodies like the strategic leader you already are—you just needed someone to show you the language. Ready to discover which of your family's symptoms are actually pointing to completely different root causes?

item.title: Why 6,988 Pages of the Tax Code Are Written to Help You Pay Less
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that 6,988 pages of the tax code exist solely to help you pay less money?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I used to think taxes were just something that happened to me — until I realized I was leaving tens of thousands on the table every year. When Barbara Schreihans broke down how the tax code actually works, it completely shifted how I think about money and business strategy.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Only 12 pages of the 7,000-page tax code tell you what to pay — the other 6,988 pages show you how to pay less.
  2. S-Corps make you 35 times less likely to be audited than sole proprietorships and can save significant self-employment taxes.
  3. Switching to an S-Corp can save you over $6,000 annually even on just $40,000 of income.
  4. Women have higher investment returns than men but invest less money overall.
  5. Your accountant should explain how to take deductions legally, not just say no to everything.
  6. Strategic December car purchases can result in 100% tax write-offs for business vehicles.
  7. Regular money conversations with your spouse prevent financial conflicts and build wealth together.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The tax code is a roadmap for entrepreneurs, not a punishment"

The big idea

Here's something that might blow your mind: out of the 7,000 pages in the tax code, only 12 pages tell you what you owe. The remaining 6,988 pages? They're literally a roadmap showing you how to pay less. Yet most of us entrepreneurs are overpaying because we don't understand this fundamental truth.

Barbara Schreihans walked me through some eye-opening numbers that every woman building a business needs to hear. If you're still operating as a sole proprietorship, you're 35 times more likely to get audited than if you switch to an S-Corp. And the savings? Even on just $40,000 of income, making that switch can save you over $6,000 annually. When you're juggling kids, a business, and everything else, that's real money that can go back into your family or your growth.

But here's what really got me fired up: women consistently outperform men when it comes to investment returns, yet we're investing less money overall. We're better at this game, but we're not playing it fully. Barbara and I dove into why this happens and what you can do about it — including having those money conversations with your spouse that most of us avoid but absolutely shouldn't.

We also talked about something practical you can implement right now: strategic timing for business purchases. That December car purchase? It could mean a 100% tax write-off if you do it right. And if your accountant's default response to deductions is 'no' without explanation, it might be time for a new accountant who understands that their job is to help you legally minimize what you pay, not maximize it.

This conversation completely changed how I think about taxes — from something that happens to me to something I can strategically plan around. If you're ready to stop overpaying and start thinking like the CEO you are, you need to hear Barbara's full breakdown and take our quiz to see where you might be leaving money on the table.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Barbara Schreihans

Barbara Schreihans is the Founder and CEO of Your Tax Coach, creator of the Write Off Your Life™ Course, and a nationally recognized tax strategist who helps entrepreneurs save tens of millions in taxes while growing their profits.

item.title: How Love and Oneness Unlock Million-Dollar Business Success with Dave Lundgren
item.content:encoded:
What if quantum physics and ancient spiritual wisdom both point to the same secret behind billion-dollar business success?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to have Dave Lundgren on the show after witnessing how he's helped build billion-dollar companies alongside Tony Robbins and Dean Graziosi using principles that sound almost too simple to be true. But when someone consistently creates those kinds of results, I pay attention—and you should too.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Love and Oneness are universal laws backed by both quantum physics and spirituality that drive true business success.
  2. Top entrepreneurs tap into feminine energy for visionary breakthroughs while using masculine energy for execution and structure.
  3. Women need approximately 20% masculine energy to effectively scale their businesses and lead with authority.
  4. Authenticity is your most powerful marketing tool because it allows you to genuinely connect with your ideal audience.
  5. Real estate investing offers four returns on every dollar invested, making it superior to traditional stock market investing.
  6. AI should be used as a tool to free you to be more human, not replace human consciousness and connection.
  7. The measure of mastery in leadership is how quickly you can recover from setbacks and challenges.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"The false sense of separation is the root cause of most human suffering in business and life."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're pushing so hard in your business but somehow still spinning your wheels? Dave Lundgren says it's because we're missing two fundamental laws that govern not just business success, but literally everything in the universe: Love and Oneness. And before you roll your eyes thinking this is going to be some woo-woo conversation, Dave backs this up with quantum physics and real-world billion-dollar results.

Here's what hit me hardest in our conversation: as women, we naturally operate from this place of connection and intuition—what Dave calls feminine energy—but we've been told that's not enough for business. The truth? That visionary, heart-centered energy is exactly what creates breakthrough success. You just need about 20% masculine energy layered in for the execution and structure. Think about it: you're already tapping into Love when you genuinely care about solving your customers' problems, and you're accessing Oneness when you trust your intuition about market timing or product decisions.

Dave also dropped some serious wisdom about authenticity being your most powerful marketing tool. Not the kind of authenticity where you overshare your morning routine, but the kind where you show up as genuinely you because that's how you magnetize your ideal audience. He's seen this principle work across industries, from real estate (where he explains why it offers four returns on every dollar versus traditional investing) to tech companies scaling to nine figures.

What really struck me was his perspective on using AI as a tool to free us to be more human, not replace our consciousness and connection. And his definition of leadership mastery? It's not about never falling down—it's about how quickly you can get back up. If you're ready to stop fighting against your natural strengths and start leveraging them for the success you deserve, you need to dive into the full conversation and discover where you stand on the leadership resilience scale.

item.title: Why Writing Your Book Alone Is Holding You Back: The Power of Book Midwives
item.description: Episode 47 | with Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski
item.content:encoded:
What if the reason you haven't finished your book isn't lack of discipline, but lack of community?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I spent years believing I needed to white-knuckle my way through writing alone in my office at 5 AM. Then I met my book midwives, Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski, and everything changed. This conversation will shift how you think about your creative process—and finally give you permission to write the way you were meant to.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Writing is an embodied act that requires community, nourishment, and permission to be messy.
  2. The defining question for any manuscript is: 'What absolutely has to be in this book?'
  3. Women write differently than men and need to honor their unique creative processes rather than forcing traditional methods.
  4. Your body knows your story before your mind does, so listen to what wants to emerge.
  5. There's never a perfect time to write a book, just like having a baby or getting a puppy.
  6. Legacy authors don't just write one book—they step into writing as part of their identity.
  7. The people you least expect will be most transformed by your words, so don't hold back your story.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"What absolutely has to be in this book? That question changed everything for my writing process."

The big idea

You've been fed a lie about writing. The myth goes like this: real writers lock themselves away, discipline themselves into productivity, and push through the resistance alone. But what if I told you that approach is actually sabotaging your book? What if the secret isn't more willpower, but more community?

In this episode, I sit down with my own book midwives—the women who helped me birth my bestseller—to explore why women write differently and how to honor your unique creative process. Dr. Shana and Dr. Cindy reveal that writing is an embodied act, not just a mental exercise. Your body holds your story before your mind even knows what it wants to say. The key is learning to listen to what wants to emerge, rather than forcing what you think should come out.

We dive deep into the one question that will transform your manuscript: 'What absolutely has to be in this book?' Not what's marketable, not what's trendy, but what absolutely must exist in the world through your voice. This shift alone will help you cut through the noise and write from your core truth. We also tackle the perfectionism trap that keeps so many women stuck—waiting for the right time, the right circumstances, the right level of expertise. Spoiler alert: there's never a perfect time to write a book, just like there's never a perfect time to have a baby or get a puppy.

Here's what really got me: the people who will be most transformed by your words are often the ones you least expect. That story you're holding back because you think it's too niche, too personal, too messy? That's exactly what someone needs to hear. Legacy authors don't just write one book and disappear—they step into writing as part of their identity, trusting that their voice matters in the conversation.

This conversation completely changed how I approach creativity, and I have a feeling it's going to unlock something powerful in you too. Ready to discover your unique writing process and finally give yourself permission to write messy, embodied, and in community?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski

Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski are the co-founders of Synergy Publishing Group and creators of the Rebel Writers program. These 'book midwives' combine decades of writing expertise with a revolutionary approach to helping mission-driven women birth their books through community, embodiment, and retreat experiences.

item.title: 50 Years Behind the Camera: What Media Insiders Don't Want You to Know
item.content:encoded:
What if everything you think you know about media credibility is actually a carefully constructed performance?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had the incredible opportunity to sit down with Steve Edwards, a broadcasting legend with five decades in the industry. His perspective on authenticity, career reinvention, and the hidden truths about media completely shifted how I think about showing up as a leader.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Gratitude is a daily superpower that can combat negative mental spirals and transform your perspective on challenges.
  2. Career longevity requires constant reinvention while staying true to your authentic self rather than performing a character.
  3. Social media is having unprecedented negative impacts on young people, particularly girls, that we haven't fully grasped yet.
  4. The internet and cable news have accelerated tribalism in ways our society hasn't caught up to, fragmenting our shared reality.
  5. Authenticity beats performance every single time, whether you're on camera or in any leadership role.
  6. Staying relevant in any career means never stopping your curiosity and willingness to learn and adapt.
  7. Breaking news coverage requires balancing truth-telling with responsibility for the audience's emotional wellbeing.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Gratitude will get you through anything - it's a daily practice that can change your entire perspective"

The big idea

When someone has spent fifty years behind the camera, you listen. Steve Edwards didn't just witness the evolution of media—he lived it, adapted through it, and came out the other side with insights that will change how you think about authenticity in your own leadership journey. This conversation revealed truths about performance versus genuine connection that every woman leader needs to hear.

Here's what struck me most: Steve's emphasis on gratitude as a daily superpower. Not the fluffy kind we see plastered on Instagram, but the real, gritty practice of shifting your mental state when everything feels like it's falling apart. He's right—gratitude isn't just nice-to-have; it's a strategic tool for combating the negative spirals that can derail our leadership effectiveness. And if you're managing teams, raising kids, or building something meaningful, you know exactly what I'm talking about.

But the conversation went deeper than mindset. Steve revealed how career longevity isn't about finding your lane and staying in it—it's about constant reinvention while never losing sight of who you authentically are. He's navigated decades of industry changes by staying curious, adapting his approach, but never performing a character. That distinction between adaptation and performance? It's everything. Whether you're in the C-suite, running your own business, or leading a team, authenticity beats performance every single time.

We also tackled some hard truths about the media landscape and what it means for how we show up as leaders in this fragmented reality. The acceleration of tribalism, the unprecedented impact on young people (especially our daughters), and the responsibility we have as leaders to model something different. Steve's insights on balancing truth-telling with emotional responsibility hit differently when you realize how much our teams and families are looking to us for stability in chaotic times.

This episode will challenge how you think about reinvention, authenticity, and the daily practices that sustain long-term success—plus there's a quiz that will help you identify which of Steve's key strategies you should implement first in your own leadership approach.

Steve Edwards is a legendary Los Angeles broadcaster with over 50 years in television, including hosting Good Day LA for 17 years. He has received the Governor's Award from the Academy of Television Arts & Sciences and has a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.

item.title: How One Mom's 'Silly' Trivia Business Became a Community Empire
item.content:encoded:
What if your 'silly' business idea could outperform every serious, prestigious venture you've considered?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I met Christina Wooldridge and immediately thought: here's someone who gets it. She turned trivia nights into a community empire, proving that the most unconventional ideas often solve the most human needs. This conversation challenged everything I thought I knew about what makes a business 'serious enough' to pursue.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Hospitality isn't a soft skill—it's a leadership superpower that creates real power in any conversation.
  2. Your business idea doesn't need to be prestigious to be valuable and profitable.
  3. Human connection, not just products or services, is what keeps customers coming back long-term.
  4. Scaling doesn't always mean doing more—sometimes it means packaging your expertise differently.
  5. Motherhood sharpens leadership skills through forced intentionality and laser focus.
  6. The real value in business comes from creating belonging and community, not just transactions.
  7. Unconventional ideas often succeed because they solve real human needs that others overlook.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Hospitality is not a soft skill — it's a leadership superpower"

The big idea

You know that business idea you've been dismissing as 'too simple' or 'not prestigious enough'? Christina Wooldridge wants you to think again. When she started her trivia business, people called it silly. Fast forward to today, and she's built something that goes far beyond games and questions—she's created a community empire that brings people together in ways that fancy corporate initiatives never could.

Here's what Christina taught me that I want you to hear: hospitality isn't some soft skill you add to your resume as an afterthought. It's a leadership superpower that creates real power in every conversation you have. When you lead with genuine care for human connection, you're not just building a business—you're building belonging. And belonging, as it turns out, is incredibly profitable because it keeps people coming back not just for what you offer, but for how you make them feel.

We talked about how motherhood actually sharpens your leadership skills through what Christina calls 'forced intentionality.' When you have limited time and energy, you get laser-focused on what truly matters. You stop wasting energy on impressive-sounding strategies that don't move the needle and start focusing on what actually creates value for real people. This isn't about doing less—it's about doing what counts.

The conversation took an interesting turn when Christina shared how she scaled not by doing more of everything, but by packaging her expertise differently. Sometimes growth looks like expansion, but sometimes it looks like depth. Sometimes it means serving the same people in new ways rather than constantly chasing new audiences. This mindset shift alone could change how you think about your next business move.

What struck me most was Christina's point about unconventional ideas succeeding precisely because they solve human needs that others overlook. While everyone else is chasing the latest trend or trying to build the next unicorn, there's real opportunity in serving the fundamental human need for connection and community. Ready to discover which of your 'silly' ideas might actually be your smartest business move?

About Christina Wooldridge

Christina Wooldridge is the founder of Trivia Goat, a live entertainment company bringing tech-enabled trivia to bars, breweries, and restaurants. With a background in hotel/restaurant management and nonprofit fundraising, she's a working mom of two based in Orange County, CA who transformed her hospitality expertise into a thriving community-building business.

item.title: Why Intentional Relationships Are the Ultimate Business Strategy
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you the one business skill AI will never master is sitting right in your pocket?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've built a nine-figure company, but honestly? None of that would've happened without the relationships I've intentionally cultivated over the years. When Scott MacGregor shared his 'extra inch' philosophy on connection, I knew you needed to hear this conversation—it's the missing piece in so many of our business strategies.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Stop trying to figure out the end result and just pour into people with genuine intention.
  2. Going the 'extra inch' with 5 seconds of additional effort creates 100x the impact in relationships.
  3. Use your phone's contact notes as a simple but powerful CRM to track meaningful details about people.
  4. Shift from networking to friendship building by genuinely caring about people as humans first.
  5. Relationships follow unpredictable paths, so trust the process rather than forcing outcomes.
  6. Choose impact over income because people remember how you made them feel, not your revenue.
  7. Daily relationship building, like athletic training, strengthens your connection muscles over time.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Scale can steal the soul"

The big idea

You know that feeling when someone remembers your kid's soccer tournament or asks about your mom's surgery three months later? That's not luck—that's intentional relationship building, and it's about to become your most powerful business advantage. While everyone else is chasing the latest AI tool or marketing hack, the women who understand this principle are quietly building empires on genuine human connection.

Here's what Scott taught me that changed everything: stop trying to engineer outcomes and start pouring into people with pure intention. It's not about networking—it's about friendship building. The difference? When you network, you're calculating ROI. When you build friendships, you're investing in humans first, business second. And here's the kicker: that 'extra inch' of effort—those five additional seconds where you jot down that her daughter just started college or he's training for his first marathon—creates 100x the impact in your relationships.

The beauty of this approach is its simplicity. Your phone already has everything you need. Those contact notes most of us ignore? They're your secret weapon. Start tracking the details that matter to the people in your life. Not their job titles or what they can do for you, but who they are as humans. Their hopes, their struggles, their wins. This isn't manipulation—it's genuine care systematized.

But here's where most of us get it wrong: we want to control the outcome. We build relationships thinking, 'If I invest in Sarah, maybe she'll refer clients to me.' Stop it. Relationships follow unpredictable paths, and the moment you try to force a specific result, you've moved from friendship back to networking. Trust the process. Choose impact over income, because people will forget your revenue numbers, but they'll never forget how you made them feel.

Think of relationship building like athletic training—it's a daily practice that strengthens your connection muscles over time. Some days you won't feel like reaching out, checking in, or going that extra inch, but that's exactly when it matters most. The women who master this skill don't just build businesses; they build legacies rooted in genuine human connection that no algorithm can replicate.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Scott MacGregor

Scott MacGregor is an entrepreneur, publisher, and 4x author with 20+ years in sales leadership. As founder of The Outlier Project and Editor-in-Chief of Outlier Magazine, he champions authentic relationship building over traditional networking.

item.title: The $10 Trillion Job Every Mother Does for Free (And Why It's Time to Name It)
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that every mother performs $10 trillion worth of unpaid labor annually—and we've been calling it love?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking about this conversation with Dr. Danielle Dowling for weeks now. As someone who's built a nine-figure company while raising six kids, I thought I understood the weight of invisible labor. But Danielle opened my eyes to something deeper—how we've been conditioned to see our overwhelm as personal failure instead of systemic injustice.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Naming motherhood as work is not a betrayal of love—it's an act of dignity and self-worth.
  2. Women globally perform 12.5 billion hours of unpaid labor daily, valued at over $10 trillion annually.
  3. Self-abandonment disguised as virtue is still self-abandonment and erases women's identities.
  4. Replace asking for 'help' with asking partners to 'own' responsibilities to transfer real accountability.
  5. The progression of self-worth to self-respect to self-care creates sustainable boundaries and wellness.
  6. Good girl conditioning transforms systemic overwhelm into personal shame instead of warranted critique.
  7. Raising independent children requires mothers to stop doing everything for them and teach responsibility.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Naming motherhood as work is not a betrayal of love—it's an act of dignity."

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're juggling seventeen things before 9 AM and someone asks what you 'do all day'? Dr. Danielle Dowling has the data to back up what you already know in your bones: the work you do as a mother isn't just valuable—it's worth over $10 trillion annually. Yet somehow, we've been convinced that naming this labor diminishes our love. Spoiler alert: it doesn't.

In this episode, Danielle breaks down the staggering reality that women globally perform 12.5 billion hours of unpaid labor daily. But here's what hit me hardest—she explains how 'good girl conditioning' has transformed what should be warranted critique of an unfair system into personal shame about our own capacity. We've been gaslighted into believing that if we can't handle everything with a smile, we're failing. The truth? The system is failing us.

Danielle walks us through practical shifts that can change everything, starting with language. Stop asking your partner to 'help' with household responsibilities and start asking them to 'own' them. It's not about being difficult—it's about transferring real accountability. She also reveals how self-abandonment disguised as virtue is still self-abandonment, and why the progression from self-worth to self-respect to self-care isn't selfish—it's essential for sustainable leadership in your home and beyond.

One of the most liberating moments comes when Danielle explains how raising truly independent children requires us to stop doing everything for them. Yes, it's harder in the short term to teach a six-year-old to pack their own lunch. But we're not doing them any favors by robbing them of competence. This episode will challenge everything you've been told about motherhood, work, and what it means to be a 'good' woman—and it comes with a quiz that will help you identify exactly where you're abandoning yourself in the name of virtue.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Danielle Dowling

Dr. Danielle Dowling, Psy.D. is a Los Angeles-based certified life coach and doctor of psychology with over 14 years and 15,000+ hours of coaching experience. She helps high-achieving women and mothers reconnect with their self-worth and build lives they genuinely feel proud of. She is the author of the upcoming book Good Girl, Bad Mom.

item.title: From Divorce to Six-Figure Success: How One Mom Rewrote Her Financial Story
item.content:encoded:
What if your biggest financial failure was actually the plot twist that led to your greatest success story?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to have Tiana Reeves on the show because her story shattered every assumption I had about what it takes to rebuild after rock bottom. Here's a woman who went from complete financial dependence to six-figure success in months, not years—and she did it all while redefining what wealth actually means.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Rich isn't about income—it's about joy, peace, and the memories you create with your family.
  2. Teaching children financial literacy early using savings, giving, and spending buckets sets them up for lifelong success.
  3. Imposter syndrome often means you haven't found your true purpose yet, not that you're inadequate.
  4. Marriage should be treated like a corporate merger—make sure your values and goals align before committing.
  5. Financial dependence costs more than money—it costs your peace, autonomy, and sense of self-worth.
  6. Starting with what you enjoy naturally leads to finding your purpose and financial success.
  7. Peace in your household is worth more than any amount of gold or material wealth.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Marriage is a corporate merger — and divorce is a divestiture. Make sure your corporate cultures align before you merge."

The big idea

You know that moment when life forces you to choose between staying small or stepping into who you're meant to become? That's exactly where Tiana Reeves found herself during her divorce—financially dependent, scared, but ready to rewrite her entire story. What happened next will challenge everything you think you know about building wealth as a working mom.

Tiana didn't just rebuild her finances—she revolutionized her entire relationship with money. She passed her PMP certification in six weeks (yes, you read that right) and discovered that true wealth isn't about your bank account balance. It's about creating joy, peace, and meaningful memories with your family. This shift in perspective didn't just change her bottom line; it transformed how she shows up as a mother, teaching her children financial literacy through simple savings, giving, and spending buckets that set them up for lifelong success.

But here's where Tiana's story gets really interesting: she learned to treat major life decisions like corporate mergers. Before committing to anything—whether it's a relationship, a job, or a business venture—she makes sure values and goals are completely aligned. This strategic approach helped her understand that financial dependence costs far more than money; it costs your peace, autonomy, and sense of self-worth. The freedom to make choices from a place of strength rather than desperation? That's priceless.

What struck me most about our conversation was Tiana's approach to finding purpose. Instead of forcing herself into someone else's definition of success, she started with what brought her natural joy and let that guide her path. She discovered that imposter syndrome often isn't about inadequacy—it's a signal that you haven't found your true calling yet. When you align with your authentic purpose, those feelings of not belonging simply melt away.

Tiana now leads a movement for corporate moms, proving that your lowest moments really can become your greatest plot twists. Her definition of household peace being worth more than gold will hit you right in the chest—and her practical strategies for achieving both financial success and family harmony will give you a clear roadmap forward. Ready to discover which money mindset might be holding you back from your own plot twist?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Tiana Reeves

Tiana Reeves is a PMP-certified project manager, Six Sigma Black Belt, and creator of the Corporate Mom Movement. She transformed her life from pregnancy loss, divorce, and corporate burnout into becoming a bestselling author and advocate for working mothers' financial independence.

item.title: From Frustrated Mom to Political Candidate: When Parental Rights Meet Leadership
item.content:encoded:
What if your mama bear instincts are actually the political leadership skills this country desperately needs right now?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot about the intersection of motherhood and civic leadership lately. When I sat down with Kristina Irwin—a mom who went from frustrated parent to state senate candidate—I realized we've been selling ourselves short on just how transferable our parenting skills really are.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Motherhood provides a masterclass in leadership skills that translate directly to political and community leadership.
  2. Parents have the right and responsibility to know what their children are being taught in schools.
  3. Write-in campaigns can be surprisingly effective when you have genuine grassroots support and a clear message.
  4. Education reform should focus on teaching practical skills like AI literacy and real-world preparation rather than ideology.
  5. Breaking political supermajorities requires common sense candidates willing to put principles over party politics.
  6. When you feel powerless about your children's future, channel that frustration into actionable change rather than helpless anger.
  7. Parental rights issues cross party lines and represent a fundamental women's issue about protecting our children.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Public education has become indoctrination"

The big idea

You know that feeling when you discover something happening with your kids that makes your blood boil? That protective instinct that kicks in when you realize the systems you trusted aren't serving your children? Kristina Irwin knows it well. When COVID lockdowns pulled back the curtain on what was actually happening in her children's classrooms, she didn't just vent to other parents or complain on social media. She decided to run for state senate.

Here's what struck me most about Kristina's story: she didn't wait for permission or credentials to step into leadership. She recognized that the skills she'd been honing as a mother—advocating for her children, managing competing priorities, building consensus among different personalities—were exactly what political leadership requires. The same intuition that tells you when something's off with your teenager is the same skill that can identify broken systems in your community.

We dove deep into the practical side of her campaign strategy, and honestly, it's brilliant in its simplicity. Kristina's running a write-in campaign focused on breaking political supermajorities with common sense solutions. She's not trying to be everything to everyone—she's laser-focused on parental rights, education reform, and giving parents real transparency into what their children are learning. Her approach to education particularly resonated with me: instead of ideological battles, why aren't we teaching kids AI literacy and real-world skills they'll actually need?

What I love most about this conversation is how Kristina reframes parental rights as a women's issue. We're not talking about partisan politics here—we're talking about mothers across the political spectrum who want to protect and guide their children. That mama bear energy you feel when someone threatens your kid? That's not anger to be suppressed or apologized for. That's leadership fuel waiting to be channeled into meaningful change.

If you've ever felt that helpless frustration watching systems fail your children, this episode will show you how to transform that energy into action that actually moves the needle—plus there's a quiz to help you identify your own pathway from frustrated parent to community leader.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kristina Irwin

Kristina Irwin is a mother, first-generation immigrant, and candidate for California State Senate District 24. She earned 6,300 write-in votes in 2022 after being awakened to educational concerns during COVID lockdowns and is now running on the official 2026 ballot on a platform of parental rights, property rights, and public safety.

item.title: Why Modern Women Are 'Out of Balance' and How to Return to Your Feminine Nature
item.content:encoded:
What if everything you've been taught about success is keeping you from accessing your true power as a woman?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've been thinking a lot lately about why so many brilliant women I know are completely exhausted, despite achieving everything they thought they wanted. When Chelsea Ann O'Brien shared her story of walking away from extreme wealth to rediscover her feminine nature, something clicked for me about what we're all really searching for.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Modern women are 'out of their nature' due to conditioning that pushes them into masculine energy patterns.
  2. The father wound drives who you date, while the mother wound drives how you mother your children.
  3. Motherhood isn't an identity to grasp but a gift that exists beyond the ego.
  4. Healing requires feeling your emotions, not thinking your way through them.
  5. Women don't need to become like men to access their power and succeed.
  6. Returning to feminine nature starts with creating space from societal conditioning.
  7. Redefining success means basing it on your authentic nature rather than external validation.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Women don't need to become like men to be powerful"

The big idea

You know that feeling when you're checking all the boxes—career advancing, family thriving, life looking picture-perfect from the outside—but something inside feels completely off? Chelsea Ann calls this being 'out of your nature,' and honestly, it explains so much about why we're all so burned out. During our conversation, she revealed how modern conditioning has pushed us into masculine energy patterns that aren't sustainable, leaving us disconnected from our intuition and our authentic power.

What really got my attention was when Chelsea talked about the difference between the father wound and mother wound—how the father wound shows up in who you choose to date, while the mother wound affects how you mother your own children. It's wild how these patterns play out without us even realizing it. She shared her own journey of having extreme wealth and then choosing to surrender it all, not because she had to, but because she was called to something deeper. That takes serious courage, and it opened up a conversation about what success actually means when it's based on your authentic nature rather than external validation.

One thing Chelsea said that I can't stop thinking about: 'Motherhood isn't an identity to grasp but a gift that exists beyond the ego.' Whether you're a mother or not, there's something profound here about how we approach the roles in our lives. Are we grasping at identities, or are we allowing ourselves to flow with what feels natural? She also dropped some serious truth about healing—that we can't think our way through everything. Sometimes we actually have to feel our emotions instead of intellectualizing them to death.

The conversation took an interesting turn when Chelsea shared what she's sensing about a major shift coming in 2027. I won't spoil it here, but it has everything to do with women returning to their feminine nature and redefining what power looks like. If you've been feeling like you're swimming upstream in your own life, this episode might just give you the permission you didn't know you needed to trust what you're sensing deep down.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Chelsea Ann O'Brien

Chelsea Ann O'Brien is a board-certified family nurse practitioner and author of five books including The Feminine Effect and Mystical Motherhood. She's a modern healer who studied with renowned midwife Ina May Gaskin and practices at The Morrison Center in NYC, dedicated to helping women remember their true nature.

item.title: Why Parenting Is Leadership (And How to Stop Doing Everything Yourself)
item.content:encoded:
What if every tantrum, bedtime negotiation, and chore chart is actually advanced leadership training?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I had to laugh when Kristina told me about crying on her closet floor, overwhelmed by everything she was trying to manage. Friend, if you've never had a closet floor moment as a mother, you're either lying or not paying attention. This conversation completely reframed how I see the work we do at home.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Your home is a leadership academy where you practice systems management, delegation, and domestic leadership daily.
  2. Playing the long game means investing in teaching skills now for independence later rather than doing everything yourself.
  3. Fifteen minutes of one-on-one time with each child daily creates transformational connection and communication.
  4. Technology should be navigated and scaffolded rather than eliminated, teaching kids to manage screen time responsibly.
  5. The hardest seasons in life often become incubators for your next chapter and greatest purpose.
  6. Accepting your child's authentic self during identity exploration strengthens your relationship long-term.
  7. Co-parenting after divorce works best when you play to each other's strengths rather than competing.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"You may have given a life, but it's not your life to live"

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about motherhood: you're already practicing advanced leadership skills every single day. When you're managing schedules, delegating chores, and navigating sibling conflicts, you're developing systems thinking that would make Fortune 500 CEOs jealous. Kristina Campos learned this the hard way, building The Impactful Parent empire from those overwhelming early days when she felt like she was drowning in domestic chaos.

The game-changer? Understanding that parenting is about playing the long game, not surviving the daily grind. Instead of doing everything yourself (hello, martyr complex), you can invest fifteen minutes of focused time with each child daily and start teaching them the skills they need to become independent humans. This isn't about creating perfect little robots—it's about building capable, confident people who don't need you to micromanage their lives forever.

What really struck me about Kristina's approach is how she handles the tough stuff without losing her mind. Technology doesn't have to be the enemy if you teach kids to manage screen time responsibly rather than banning it completely. Identity exploration during those terrifying teenage years becomes less scary when you focus on accepting your child's authentic self. Even co-parenting after divorce can work beautifully when you stop competing and start playing to each other's strengths.

The truth is, your hardest seasons often become the foundation for your greatest purpose. Kristina's closet floor breakdown led to helping thousands of parents discover their own leadership potential. Your current chaos might be preparing you for something bigger than you can imagine. Ready to discover what kind of leader you already are and how to leverage those skills both at home and beyond?

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Kristina Campos

Kristina Campos is the founder of The Impactful Parent with 20+ years of classroom experience from Pre-K through 12th grade. A mother of four and parenting educator, she created a coaching practice, podcast, and free parenting app after rebuilding her life following divorce.

item.title: Why Self-Care Isn't Selfish: Dr. Laura Brayton on Holistic Motherhood
item.content:encoded:
What if everything you've been told about 'having it all' as a mother is actually keeping you from thriving?

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I knew I had to have Dr. Laura Brayton on the show when I realized how many high-achieving women are burning out trying to be everything to everyone—except themselves. This conversation shifted something fundamental in how I think about motherhood and leadership, and I have a feeling it's going to do the same for you.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Self-care is not selfish—it's mandatory for every mother because everyone benefits when mom is well.
  2. Chiropractic care is safe throughout all three trimesters and can significantly improve pregnancy outcomes.
  3. Preconception detox should begin six months before conception for both partners to optimize fertility and baby's health.
  4. Your nervous system must be regulated to lead from intuition rather than fear-based decisions.
  5. Life is written in pencil—you have permission to pivot your career to align with motherhood.
  6. Being fully present for one hour daily gives your children more attention than most kids receive.
  7. Corporate skills translate beautifully into household leadership when you embrace the shift in energy.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Self-care is not selfish—it is mandatory for every mother"

The big idea

Here's what nobody tells you about being a mother who leads: you can't pour from an empty cup, but you've been conditioned to believe that filling your own cup first is somehow selfish. Dr. Laura Brayton dismantles this myth completely. As she puts it, self-care isn't selfish—it's mandatory, because everyone in your orbit benefits when you're operating from a place of wellness rather than depletion.

We dive deep into what holistic motherhood actually looks like in practice. Laura walks through why preconception preparation should start six months before you even try to conceive (and yes, your partner needs to detox too), how chiropractic care throughout pregnancy can dramatically improve outcomes, and why regulating your nervous system is the secret to making decisions from intuition rather than fear. This isn't about adding more to your already full plate—it's about fundamentally shifting how you approach your well-being.

But here's where it gets really practical for those of us juggling leadership roles: Laura breaks down how your corporate skills translate beautifully into household leadership when you stop fighting the shift in energy and start embracing it. She gives permission for what many of us secretly wonder—that it's okay to pivot your career to align with motherhood, because life is written in pencil. And she drops this truth bomb that every working mother needs to hear: being fully present for just one hour daily gives your children more focused attention than most kids receive all day.

This episode is for every woman who's been told she needs to choose between being an exceptional mother and an exceptional leader. Laura proves you can be both, but only when you stop treating your own well-being as optional. Ready to discover how holistic motherhood becomes the foundation for everything else in your life?

About Dr. Laura Brayton

Dr. Laura Brayton is a chiropractor, pediatric specialist, and functional medicine practitioner with over 20 years of practice in Hoboken, NJ. She hosts the Well-Adjusted Mama podcast and specializes in empowering women through conscious conception, healthy pregnancy, and joyful motherhood using holistic approaches including the Webster Technique for optimal birth outcomes.

item.title: Why Parenting and Leadership Are the Same Skill Set (According to a Psychologist)
item.content:encoded:
A clinical psychologist just proved that your parenting skills are identical to your leadership skills.

Hey friend,

In this week's episode of The Manage Her® Podcast, I've always suspected there was overlap between managing my six kids and leading my company, but I had no idea how scientifically identical these skill sets actually are. Dr. Andrea Mata's research completely reframed how I think about both roles—and why we've been getting gentle parenting (and gentle leadership) all wrong.

🎬 Watch the full episode · 🎧 Spotify · 🍎 Apple Podcasts

🔑 What you'll take away

  1. Authoritative parenting combines high expectations with high support, creating the best outcomes for children and employees.
  2. Gentle parenting swung too far from authoritarian into permissiveness, creating a generation struggling with adaptability.
  3. It costs twice an employee's salary to fire and replace them, making coaching the smarter financial investment.
  4. Gen Z employees need clear behavioral expectations and frequent check-ins, not vague annual reviews.
  5. Seventy-one percent of household cognitive labor falls on mothers, creating an unsustainable mental load.
  6. Motherhood is leadership training for every future relationship your child will have.
  7. You don't have to be perfect as a parent or leader—you have to be present and consistent.

If you're enjoying The Manage Her®, would you share it with one woman who needs to hear this? 👇

Share The Manage Her®

"Parenting your child IS modeling leadership for every future relationship"

The big idea

Here's what no one tells you about leadership development: you might already be getting world-class training every single day at home. Dr. Andrea Mata, a clinical psychologist, walked me through research that shows parenting and leadership require the exact same psychological toolkit—and most of us are unknowingly sabotaging both by swinging too far toward 'gentle' approaches that lack structure.

The magic formula? Authoritative leadership, whether you're dealing with a toddler tantrum or an underperforming employee. This means combining high expectations with high support—not the permissive approach that gentle parenting often becomes. Dr. Mata explained how we've overcorrected from the authoritarian style our parents used, creating a generation that struggles with adaptability because they've never had to navigate clear boundaries or consequences. The same thing is happening in our companies with Gen Z employees who desperately need frequent check-ins and explicit behavioral expectations, not annual reviews and vague feedback.

The financial case for better leadership is staggering: it costs twice an employee's salary to fire and replace them, making coaching and clear expectations a smart business investment. But here's what hit me hardest—seventy-one percent of household cognitive labor falls on mothers, creating an unsustainable mental load that's training us to be hyper-efficient leaders while burning us out. We're essentially running the most complex operations imaginable (families) while society tells us it doesn't count as 'real' leadership experience.

Dr. Mata's perspective on perfectionism changed everything for me. You don't have to be perfect as a parent or leader—you have to be present and consistent. Every interaction you have with your children is leadership training for every future relationship they'll have with bosses, partners, and their own kids. The pressure we put on ourselves to get it exactly right is actually counterproductive to building the resilience and adaptability our kids and employees need to thrive in an uncertain world.

This conversation will shift how you see both your role at home and at work—plus there's a quiz that reveals which leadership style you default to and why it might be sabotaging your results in both areas.

👉 Read the full show notes + take the self-discovery quiz →

About Dr. Andrea Mata

Dr. Andrea Mata (Dr. Dre) is a clinical psychologist, national speaker, and founder of BrightSpot Families. She's the author of The Number Two Parenting Book and graduated summa cum laude from Valparaiso University with her doctorate from Kent State.
